proposals.title: Equilibrium SPME as a Direct Replacement for Exhaustive Solvent Extraction in Regulatory Analysis: A Validation Study for Priority Contaminants in Soil
proposals.motivation: Regulatory environmental and food safety testing mandates exhaustive solvent extraction (e.g., EPA, FDA methods), which is time-consuming, solvent-intensive, and measures total content, not the bioavailable fraction. Solid-phase microextraction (SPME) is a well-established equilibrium technique that measures freely dissolved concentration, but its acceptance for compliance testing is hindered by the perception that it cannot provide equivalent quantitative data to exhaustive methods. Demonstrating this equivalence is critical for regulatory adoption of greener, faster analytical tools.
proposals.hypothesis: For a suite of model regulatory analytes (e.g., PAHs, pesticides) in complex solid matrices, equilibrium SPME using calibrated, matrix-compatible coatings will yield quantitative results statistically equivalent to those obtained by standard exhaustive extraction protocols, thereby challenging the necessity of total extraction for compliance decisions.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential SPME approach using biocompatible polyacrylonitrile or hydrophilic-lipophilic balance (HLB) particles in a binder to mitigate coating fouling and analyte displacement in complex soil samples. Calibration will be performed via equilibrium extraction with isotope-labeled internal standards and the kinetic calibration approach (e.g., using standard-free kinetic calibration). The SPME results will be directly compared to data from validated Soxhlet or pressurized liquid extraction (PLE) methods followed by GC-MS/MS analysis. Statistical equivalence will be assessed using paired t-tests and Bland-Altman analysis at relevant regulatory thresholds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, method-comparison dataset to advocate for the revision of standard regulatory methods to include equilibrium-based SPME. It will deliver a validated, high-throughput, and solvent-minimized protocol for determining contaminant levels in solids, aligning analytical practice with green chemistry principles. The study aims to shift the regulatory paradigm from measuring 'total' to measuring 'relevant' concentration.
proposals.novelty: While SPME is known for equilibrium measurements, its direct, statistically validated substitution for exhaustive extraction in formal regulatory contexts is unexplored. The novelty lies in strategically applying recent Pawliszyn group advances in sequential SPME and robust calibration to definitively challenge a foundational assumption of compliance testing.

proposals.title: High-Density, Field-Deployable SPME Arrays with Mixed-Mode Coatings for Spatial Mapping of Bioavailable Hydrophilic and Ionizable Contaminants in Aquatic Systems
proposals.motivation: The environmental risk of polar, ionizable contaminants (e.g., pharmaceuticals, modern pesticides) is dictated by their freely dissolved, bioavailable fraction, which is poorly characterized at field scales. Traditional passive samplers are often limited for hydrophilic/ionizable analytes, and discrete grab sampling fails to capture spatial heterogeneity. There is a critical need for a high-resolution, in-situ sampling tool to map chemical activity gradients and identify exposure hotspots for accurate risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: That large-scale arrays of SPME fibers, coated with novel mixed-mode or hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced (HLB) sorbents, can provide quantitative, spatially resolved maps of the freely dissolved concentration and chemical activity of bioavailable hydrophilic and ionizable contaminants in complex aquatic environments.
proposals.method: We will develop novel SPME coatings using mixed-mode sorbents (e.g., combining weak cation/anion exchange with reversed-phase mechanisms) and biocompatible binders to efficiently sequester a broad spectrum of ionizable pharmaceuticals and pesticides. These coatings will be applied to high-density fiber arrays integrated into a field-deployable, depth-resolvable housing. The arrays will be deployed in gradients (e.g., wastewater effluent plumes) and analyzed via direct coupling to liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) using a microfluidic open interface (MOI) for high-throughput. On-fiber calibration, accounting for local pH and ionic strength effects on sorption, will enable quantification of freely dissolved concentrations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first field-deployable, high-resolution spatial mapping tool for the bioavailable fraction of challenging hydrophilic/ionizable contaminants. It will generate unprecedented datasets on exposure gradients, directly informing ecological and human health risk models. The methodology extends the applicability domain of SPME-based passive sampling to a critically important class of pollutants.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of large-scale, high-density SPME array deployment with purpose-designed mixed-mode coatings specifically targets the in-situ spatial mapping of bioavailable polar and ionizable contaminants, a capability not addressed by existing commercial SPME fibers or common passive samplers like POCIS.

proposals.title: A Closed-System, Holistic SPME-GC-MS Workflow for the Quantitative Analysis of Ultra-Hydrophobic Contaminants (log KOW > 5) in Complex Matrices
proposals.motivation: The quantitative analysis of ultra-hydrophobic organic compounds (UHOCs), such as high-molecular-weight PAHs, PCBs, and certain pesticides, remains analytically challenging. Their strong propensity for non-specific adsorption to active surfaces (e.g., glass, metal, septa) during sample handling and introduction leads to significant analyte loss, poor recovery, and irreproducible data. This undermines the accuracy of environmental monitoring, food safety testing, and toxicokinetic studies, where reliable quantification is critical for risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a tailored SPME extraction phase with a fully coordinated, inert sample introduction pathway, we can create a closed-system workflow that minimizes post-extraction analyte losses, thereby enabling the accurate and precise quantification of UHOCs.
proposals.method: The method will develop and validate a holistic workflow. First, we will synthesize and characterize SPME fibers with thick, highly retentive biocompatible coatings (e.g., C18/polyacrylonitrile or specialized polymers) optimized for UHOC extraction kinetics and capacity. Second, we will implement a fully inert pathway using deactivated liners, transfer lines, and a solvent-assisted programmed-temperature vaporizing (PTV) inlet. The workflow will be systematically tested using model UHOCs (log KOW 5-9) in complex matrices (e.g., fatty tissue, sediment extracts), comparing performance against conventional open-handling methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, end-to-end protocol that directly addresses the primary limitation of surface adsorption for UHOC analysis. It will provide the analytical community with a robust framework for obtaining reliable quantitative data, enhancing the utility of SPME for regulatory and research applications involving persistent, bioaccumulative toxins. The systematic study of loss points will also inform future interface design.
proposals.novelty: While individual components (e.g., PTV, deactivated liners) exist, the novelty lies in the deliberate, systematic engineering and validation of a *complete, closed* SPME workflow—from fiber coating to GC-MS detection—specifically designed to combat losses for UHOCs, moving beyond incremental optimization of isolated steps.

proposals.title: Quantitative Analysis of Highly Hydrophobic Compounds (log KOW > 5) Using Sequential SPME with Biocompatible, Hydrophobic-Rich Coatings to Overcome Displacement and Kinetic Limitations
proposals.motivation: The quantitative analysis of very hydrophobic organic compounds (log KOW > 5), such as persistent organic pollutants and certain pharmaceuticals, remains challenging for Solid-Phase Microextraction (SPME). These analytes suffer from slow diffusion kinetics and significant displacement effects in complex matrices, leading to biased measurements of free concentration. Addressing these limitations is critical for accurate environmental monitoring and bioanalytical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing sequential SPME with novel, highly hydrophobic, biocompatible coating materials will minimize displacement effects and improve extraction kinetics for compounds with log KOW > 5, enabling their reliable and quantitative determination.
proposals.method: The project will synthesize and characterize new SPME fiber coatings by incorporating highly hydrophobic polymers (e.g., polyacrylonitrile-based) and novel binders to create a robust, thick-phase coating optimized for hydrophobic analytes. We will systematically test these coatings using standard solutions of high log KOW compounds (e.g., PCBs, PAHs) to establish extraction kinetics and capacity. The core protocol will involve sequential SPME extractions from complex samples (e.g., fish tissue homogenate) to quantify and correct for displacement, coupled directly to mass spectrometry via a microfluidic open interface for high-throughput analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, optimized SPME protocol specifically for ultra-hydrophobic compounds, including new coating formulations and a sequential extraction method to correct for displacement. It will expand the quantitative applicability of SPME to a critical class of environmentally and biologically relevant analytes that are currently difficult to measure accurately.
proposals.novelty: While sequential SPME has been explored for biocompatible coatings, its targeted application to overcome the specific kinetic and displacement challenges of compounds with log KOW > 5 using purpose-designed, hydrophobic-rich coatings is novel and addresses a known gap in SPME methodology.

proposals.title: A Nonlinear Mixed-Effects Chemometric Model for Correcting Matrix Effects in Solid-Phase Microextraction Analysis of Environmental Samples
proposals.motivation: Quantitative analysis of organic contaminants in complex environmental matrices (e.g., soil, wastewater) using SPME is hindered by matrix effects, which unpredictably alter analyte-fiber partitioning and lead to significant quantification errors. Current approaches, such as standard addition or matrix-matched calibration, are labor-intensive and impractical for high-throughput analysis of diverse, unknown samples. A robust, generalizable calibration method is critically needed to unlock the full quantitative potential of SPME in environmental monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the variability in SPME analyte uptake caused by environmental matrix components can be modeled analogously to pharmacokinetic variability in a patient population, where a core partitioning behavior (fixed effect) is modulated by sample-specific interferences (random effects).
proposals.method: We will develop a nonlinear mixed-effects (NLME) model using the NONMEM algorithm framework. Calibration data will be generated by performing SPME (using biocompatible coatings like polyacrylonitrile) on a diverse, well-characterized library of environmental matrices spiked with target analytes (e.g., pesticides, pharmaceuticals). The model's fixed effects will describe the fundamental thermodynamics of analyte-coating partitioning, while random effects will quantify the variance attributed to specific matrix parameters (e.g., dissolved organic carbon, ionic strength, pH). Model performance will be validated by predicting concentrations in new, uncharacterized environmental samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first chemometric framework to explicitly deconvolute and correct for matrix effects in SPME, moving beyond descriptive methods to a predictive, mechanistic model. It will deliver a software tool for more accurate environmental quantification without perfect matrix matching, enhancing the reliability of SPME for regulatory and research applications. The approach also establishes a novel paradigm for treating sample populations in analytical chemistry.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and methodological translation of population pharmacokinetic modeling—a well-established tool for handling biological variability—to the domain of environmental analytical chemistry, a cross-disciplinary analogy not previously applied to SPME calibration.

proposals.title: Bridging Passive Sampling and Bioavailability: A Chemometric-Biotic Calibration Framework for SPME Using Fish Bioconcentration Data
proposals.motivation: Accurate assessment of the bioavailable fraction of contaminants in complex environmental matrices remains a critical challenge for ecological risk assessment. While Solid-Phase Microextraction (SPME) is a powerful passive sampling tool, its quantitative interpretation is often confounded by matrix effects that alter fiber uptake kinetics and equilibrium. Existing calibration methods (e.g., equilibrium sampling, kinetic models) require simplifying assumptions that may not hold in heterogeneous field samples, limiting the direct translation of chemical measurements to biological exposure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multivariate chemometric models, trained on SPME fiber extraction profiles against a reference dataset of known fish bioconcentration factors (BCFs), can correct for complex matrix effects and directly predict the bioavailable contaminant fraction and potential for bioconcentration in new environmental samples.
proposals.method: We will compile a curated dataset of published fish BCFs and corresponding water concentrations for a suite of priority organic contaminants (e.g., PAHs, PCBs, pesticides). Laboratory experiments will expose biocompatible SPME coatings (e.g., hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced, C18 with binder) to standardized solutions and complex environmental matrices (sediment porewater, wastewater) spiked with the target analytes. The resulting SPME extraction profiles (e.g., mass loadings, kinetic uptake curves) will serve as multivariate descriptors. Partial Least Squares (PLS) regression models will be trained to correlate these SPME profiles with the log-transformed BCF reference values. Model performance will be validated using independent test sets and field samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel calibration framework that directly links SPME measurements to a key ecotoxicological endpoint—bioconcentration. It will provide a robust, data-driven method to correct for matrix effects, moving beyond traditional one-analyte equilibrium assumptions. The outcome is a practical tool to estimate bioavailable concentrations and screen for bioaccumulation potential from a single SPME analysis, enhancing the utility of passive sampling in environmental monitoring.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its fusion of established biotic reference data (BCFs) with chemometric modeling of SPME profiles for matrix correction, treating the living organism as the ultimate calibration standard. This differs fundamentally from prior physical/chemical calibration methods and adapts multivariate food chemistry techniques (e.g., for authenticity testing) to solve a core environmental analytical problem.

proposals.title: Chemometric Correction of Matrix Effects in Environmental Solid-Phase Microextraction Using Multivariate Calibration Models Adapted from Food Analysis
proposals.motivation: Quantitative analysis of trace organic contaminants in complex environmental solids (e.g., soils, sediments, sludge) using SPME is severely hampered by unpredictable matrix effects, which alter analyte partitioning and lead to inaccurate determinations of free concentrations. Current approaches, such as equilibrium or kinetic calibration, require specific experimental setups or isotopically labeled standards, limiting their broad applicability. The food analysis field has a rich history of successfully using chemometrics to model and correct for complex matrix interferences in spectroscopic and chromatographic data, a resource largely untapped for SPME in environmental matrices.
proposals.hypothesis: Multivariate calibration models (e.g., Partial Least Squares, Principal Component Regression), developed and validated for correcting matrix effects in food analysis, can be successfully adapted and applied to raw SPME-MS signal data to quantitatively correct for matrix-induced biases in the analysis of organic contaminants in solid environmental samples.
proposals.method: We will develop a method using sequential SPME with biocompatible coatings (e.g., PAN, C18-PAN) to generate a comprehensive dataset of extracted ion chromatogram signals for target analytes (e.g., PAHs, pesticides) spiked into a diverse library of characterized environmental solids. Reference concentrations will be established via exhaustive extraction. This dataset will be used to train and validate chemometric models, specifically Partial Least Squares (PLS) regression and Support Vector Regression (SVR), correlating the multivariate SPME-MS signal profile with the true concentration, explicitly modeling the matrix interference as a latent variable. Model performance will be compared against traditional internal standard and standard addition calibration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, data-driven calibration framework for SPME that directly corrects for matrix effects, moving beyond reliance on physical models of partitioning. It will establish a new application domain for chemometrics within the SPME field, specifically for solid samples. The outcome will be a more universally applicable and high-throughput quantitative protocol for environmental analysis using direct SPME-MS coupling.
proposals.novelty: While chemometrics is standard in food analysis, its direct application to correct raw instrumental signals from SPME for environmental solid matrix effects is novel. This approach creatively cross-pollinates methodologies from a mature field (food chemistry) to solve a persistent problem in another (environmental SPME), leveraging the high-dimensional data from modern SPME-MS interfaces.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput Robotic SPME Platform for Regulatory Environmental and Food Safety Analysis: Method Translation and Validation
proposals.motivation: High-throughput screening is critical in regulatory labs for environmental monitoring and food safety, but traditional methods like liquid-liquid extraction are labor-intensive. While automated SPME with multi-fiber devices and robotic autosamplers has been successfully implemented in clinical toxicology for rapid drug screening, this advanced automation has not been systematically transferred to other high-volume regulatory domains. Adapting this proven clinical workflow could dramatically increase sample throughput and reduce analyst time in these essential public health and safety sectors.
proposals.hypothesis: That a robotic autosampler and multi-fiber SPME system, configured with coatings optimized for environmental and food contaminants, can achieve quantitative performance comparable to standard methods while significantly increasing throughput and operational efficiency for regulatory testing.
proposals.method: The project will adapt a commercial robotic autosampler (e.g., CTC PAL3 or Gerstel MPS) integrated with a multi-fiber SPME holder for 5-10 simultaneous extractions. We will select and validate biocompatible solid-phase coatings (e.g., mixed-mode, C18, or novel polymeric binders) suitable for target analytes like pesticides, PAHs, and mycotoxins. A key focus will be developing and optimizing a sequential SPME protocol to mitigate displacement effects in complex matrices (e.g., soil extracts, food homogenates). Method validation will follow ICH/FDA guidelines, comparing throughput, accuracy, and precision against established manual SPME and extraction-based protocols.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully validated, automated SPME platform protocol specifically for environmental and food safety labs, providing a clear roadmap for technology transfer from clinical applications. It will establish performance benchmarks for high-throughput regulatory SPME, demonstrating reduced solvent use, faster analysis times, and robust quantitative data, thereby promoting wider adoption of green analytical chemistry in compliance settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the individual components but in the systematic translation and re-engineering of a complete high-throughput SPME workflow from clinical toxicology to distinct regulatory fields with different analyte classes and matrix challenges, explicitly addressing displacement artifacts through sequential extraction protocols.

proposals.title: High-Resolution Spatial Mapping of Bioavailable Contaminants in Aquatic Systems Using Deployable, Sequential SPME Fiber Arrays
proposals.motivation: Environmental monitoring of water bodies requires understanding the spatial heterogeneity of freely dissolved, bioavailable contaminant concentrations, which is critical for accurate risk assessment. Current methods, including grab sampling or single passive samplers, lack the spatial resolution and throughput needed to capture dynamic concentration gradients and identify point sources or contaminant plumes effectively. Scaling up Solid-Phase Microextraction (SPME) for simultaneous, in-situ sampling at multiple points addresses this fundamental gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deploying large-scale arrays of SPME fibers, functionalized with sequential, biocompatible coatings to mitigate displacement effects, will enable quantitative, high-resolution spatial mapping of time-weighted average concentrations of diverse organic contaminants in aquatic environments.
proposals.method: The project will develop a robust, field-deployable array housing multiple SPME fibers. Fibers will be coated with a sequential, multi-phase system (e.g., a hydrophilic binder/lipid bilayer) based on recent Pawliszyn group advances to ensure quantitative performance for complex mixtures. Arrays will be deployed on a programmable grid. Post-deployment, fibers will be analyzed using automated, high-throughput thermal desorption coupled directly to mass spectrometry via a microfluidic open interface (MOI), enabling rapid determination of contaminant profiles. Spatial data will be processed and visualized using geostatistical kriging algorithms to generate contaminant distribution maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel paradigm for environmental forensics by providing a practical tool for generating two-dimensional concentration maps of bioavailable contaminants. It will directly translate the quantitative rigor of laboratory SPME methods to field-scale monitoring, offering regulators and scientists unprecedented spatial data for source identification and exposure modeling. The integrated system from field deployment to high-throughput MS analysis represents a significant advancement in environmental analytical chemistry.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic scaling of SPME from a single-fiber analytical tool to a coordinated, high-density spatial sampling array, explicitly designed for quantitative field mapping. This integrates recent innovations in biocompatible sequential coatings and direct MS coupling into a unified, large-scale environmental monitoring platform, a concept not yet realized in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: In Situ Deconvolution of Bioavailable Mixture Toxicity: An Inverse Modeling Framework Coupling SPME Kinetics and Baseline Toxic Pressure
proposals.motivation: Environmental risk assessment struggles with complex, dynamic contaminant mixtures where bioavailability and joint toxic effects are unknown. Traditional chemical analysis fails to capture the bioavailable fraction and integrated toxic potential of these evolving mixtures in situ. A method to directly link in-situ chemical exposure dynamics to non-specific ecological effects is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: The uptake kinetics of a complex chemical mixture onto a biocompatible SPME fiber, deployed directly in an environmental matrix, contains sufficient information to inversely model the combined, time-varying bioavailable concentration and predict its baseline toxicity.
proposals.method: We will deploy SPME fibers with hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced (HLB) or C18-bonded biocompatible coatings in dynamic systems (e.g., flow-through sediment columns). Time-series SPME extracts will be analyzed via our microfluidic open interface coupled to high-resolution mass spectrometry. An inverse modeling framework will be developed, combining a kinetic model for competitive uptake onto the fiber with a toxicokinetic-toxicodynamic model. The algorithm will iteratively fit the kinetic data to deconvolute the summed bioavailable concentration and estimate the mixture's baseline toxic pressure using the concentration addition model for non-specific effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel analytical framework for direct, in-situ assessment of the integrated bioavailable burden and its toxic potential from complex mixtures. It will establish a mechanistic link between SPME kinetics and mixture toxicity, moving beyond targeted analysis towards effect-based monitoring. The outcome is a practical tool for evaluating how environmental transformation processes alter the true exposure and risk of contaminant profiles.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the inverse modeling of SPME kinetic data to deconvolute a combined bioavailable signal and predict toxicity, rather than quantifying individual compounds. This shifts the paradigm from targeted chemical analysis to direct measurement of the integrated, toxicologically relevant exposure.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Bioavailable Fraction of Complex Chemical Mixtures and Their Transformation Products in Environmental Waters Using Sequential Multi-Phase SPME
proposals.motivation: Environmental risk assessment is challenged by the complexity of chemical mixtures and their dynamic transformation in the environment. Traditional exhaustive extraction methods overestimate exposure by measuring total concentrations, not the bioavailable fraction that drives toxicity and uptake. A pre-2026 gap exists in methods to simultaneously measure the freely dissolved concentration (Cfree) of parent compounds and their often more polar, unknown transformation products in real-world, multi-contaminant scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: That a sequential SPME approach, utilizing a suite of biocompatible coatings with varied affinities, can accurately quantify the bioavailable fraction of diverse organic contaminants and their transformation products in complex environmental matrices, accounting for displacement and competitive binding effects.
proposals.method: We will develop a method using sequential SPME fibers with distinct, biocompatible coatings (e.g., hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced, mixed-mode, and specialized polymeric phases) to selectively extract compounds across a wide polarity range. These fibers will be exposed to real environmental water samples (e.g., wastewater effluent, surface water) spiked with model contaminant mixtures (e.g., pharmaceuticals, pesticides). Direct coupling to high-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) via a microfluidic open interface will enable non-targeted screening for transformation products. Quantification will be achieved using kinetic calibration and isotopically labeled standards, with data analysis leveraging in-house algorithms for peak alignment, molecular feature detection, and trend analysis to distinguish parent compounds from transformation products based on extraction kinetics and ionization patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, high-throughput analytical framework for measuring environmentally relevant (bioavailable) concentrations of chemical mixtures and their transformation products. It will advance the field of passive sampling by explicitly addressing mixture effects and expanding applicability to polar metabolites. The resulting data will offer a more accurate basis for environmental fate modeling and ecological risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, sequential application of multiple SPME phases specifically designed to capture the broad chemical space of mixtures *and* their transformation products in a single analytical workflow, moving beyond targeted analysis of single contaminants to a comprehensive exposure assessment paradigm.

proposals.title: Quantitative Risk Assessment of Priority Contaminants in Complex Matrices Using Sequential SPME with Biocompatible Coatings and Direct MS Coupling
proposals.motivation: Environmental and biological monitoring increasingly detects complex mixtures of contaminants, but traditional risk assessment is hampered by analytical artifacts like displacement in solid-phase microextraction (SPME) and the inability to measure freely dissolved concentrations (C_free), the toxicologically relevant fraction. This gap prevents accurate hazard evaluation for human health and ecosystems. A robust, high-throughput analytical framework is needed to translate detection data into reliable risk metrics.
proposals.hypothesis: That the integration of sequential SPME using biocompatible coatings to correct for displacement effects, with direct coupling to mass spectrometry via a microfluidic open interface (MOI), will yield accurate C_free data sufficient for performing formal, quantitative risk assessments that reflect true bioavailable contaminant fractions.
proposals.method: The method will employ sequential SPME fibers coated with biocompatible materials (e.g., hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced particles in a stable binder) to perform exhaustive extraction and generate binding isotherms, correcting for displacement in complex samples like brain tissue or wastewater. The optimized SPME protocol will be directly coupled to a mass spectrometer using an improved microfluidic open interface for high-throughput analysis. The resulting C_free data for priority contaminants (e.g., PFAS, pharmaceuticals, endocannabinoids) will be input into established risk assessment models, calculating hazard quotients (HQs) and risk characterization ratios (RCRs) by comparing measured concentrations to pre-2026 toxicity reference values.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated analytical pipeline that directly bridges advanced SPME quantification with formal risk assessment protocols. It will deliver a novel dataset of risk-based priorities for contaminants in challenging matrices, moving beyond mere detection to actionable hazard evaluation. The project will demonstrate how next-generation microextraction tools can be operationalized for regulatory and public health decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While SPME is established for analysis, its sequential use with biocompatible coatings to generate data explicitly for quantitative risk assessment is novel. The direct integration of a displacement-corrected, high-throughput SPME-MS platform with ecological and human health risk calculation frameworks represents a significant, non-obvious advancement in analytical environmental chemistry.

proposals.title: Quantitative Static Headspace SPME for Complex Matrices: A Systematic Study of Coating Performance and Interface Design to Mitigate Fouling and Displacement
proposals.motivation: Direct immersion SPME is often unsuitable for complex, 'dirty' samples like crude oil, wastewater sludge, or biological tissue homogenates, as matrix components foul the fiber coating, altering its extraction properties and reducing reproducibility. While headspace (HS) SPME offers a cleaner alternative, its quantitative application for less volatile analytes in such matrices remains challenging due to potential displacement effects and a lack of systematic coating evaluation for HS-specific performance.
proposals.hypothesis: That by employing a sequential SPME approach with biocompatible, high-affinity coating materials and coupling it to a robust, high-throughput microfluidic open interface (MOI) for mass spectrometry, we can achieve accurate, quantitative static headspace analysis of semi-volatile and non-volatile analytes in complex, dirty samples while eliminating coating fouling.
proposals.method: We will systematically test a panel of SPME coatings, including novel biocompatible polymers and mixed-mode phases, for their headspace extraction efficiency and resistance to displacement effects using sequential extraction protocols. A key innovation will be the direct coupling of the HS-SPME fiber to a mass spectrometer via an improved MOI, designed to minimize dead volume and facilitate rapid thermal desorption of less volatile analytes extracted from the headspace. Method validation will involve spiked complex matrices (e.g., soil, liver homogenate, industrial effluent) with isotopically labeled standards to assess accuracy, precision, and limits of detection.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a validated, quantitative HS-SPME workflow for dirty samples, expanding the application domain of SPME to previously intractable matrices. It will provide a critical performance database for coating selection in HS mode and demonstrate a direct-MS interface optimized for the rapid analysis of less volatile compounds extracted from the headspace, enhancing throughput.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted adaptation of sequential SPME and biocompatible coatings—recently developed for direct immersion bioanalysis—specifically for static headspace applications, combined with a purpose-optimized MOI-MS interface to address the analytical gap for semi-volatiles in complex samples.

proposals.title: Quantitative Analysis of Trace Organic Contaminants in Complex Environmental Matrices Using Sequential Solid-Phase Microextraction with Biocompatible Coatings
proposals.motivation: Accurate quantification of trace organic contaminants (e.g., pharmaceuticals, pesticides) in complex environmental samples like industrial wastewater and soil extracts is critical for risk assessment but remains analytically challenging. Pre-2026, conventional SPME methods suffer from displacement and matrix effects in such samples, leading to biased results. Validating a robust SPME method for these matrices is essential for reliable environmental monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: The application of sequential SPME with biocompatible coating materials (e.g., hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced particles in a biocompatible binder) will effectively mitigate displacement and competitive binding effects, enabling accurate, matrix-effect-free quantification of target analytes in industrial wastewater and soil extracts.
proposals.method: We will apply the sequential SPME protocol, recently developed for biofluids, to industrial wastewater and soil pore water extracts. Specific biocompatible coatings (C18-PAN, HLB-PAN) will be used in a multi-fiber exposure sequence. Analysis will be performed via SPME-GC-MS, with quantification using kinetic calibration or standard addition to account for the complex matrix. Method validation will include assessment of linearity, precision, accuracy (via spike/recovery), and limits of detection in the target matrices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical protocol for the quantitative analysis of organic contaminants in some of the most challenging environmental samples. It will demonstrate the transferability of advanced biocompatible SPME strategies from bioanalysis to environmental chemistry, expanding the toolkit for reliable trace analysis.
proposals.novelty: While sequential SPME with biocompatible coatings has been explored for biological samples, its systematic application and validation for complex environmental matrices like industrial wastewater, where co-extracted interferents are vastly different, is novel. This bridges a gap between cutting-edge bioanalytical SPME methods and pressing environmental analytical needs.

proposals.title: Chemometric Optimization of Solid-Phase Microextraction: A Multivariate Analysis of Coating, Binder, and Phase Variables on Analyte Displacement and Recovery
proposals.motivation: Solid-phase microextraction (SPME) method development is complex, involving interdependent variables like coating chemistry, binder composition, and extraction phase geometry, which collectively influence analyte displacement and quantitative accuracy. Current optimization relies on univariate or limited factorial designs, which fail to capture the full interaction landscape, leading to suboptimal methods and unpredictable performance in complex matrices like biological tissue.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that principal component analysis (PCA) and partial least squares (PLS) regression can systematically deconvolute the complex, non-linear interactions between key SPME variables (coating type, binder ratio, coating thickness) and their combined effect on analyte recovery and displacement phenomena, enabling predictive model-based optimization.
proposals.method: We will design a structured experiment varying coating material (e.g., PDMS, C18, mixed-mode), binder concentration, and physical coating dimensions. Extractions will be performed on standardized samples spiked with analytes of varying hydrophobicity. The resulting multivariate data (extraction efficiency, displacement ratios, kinetics) will be analyzed using PCA for exploratory pattern recognition and variable influence projection. Subsequently, PLS regression will be employed to build quantitative models predicting extraction performance from the experimental variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational chemometric framework for rational SPME method development, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will yield interpretable models that clarify how coating composition and geometry interact to control selectivity and mitigate displacement, directly supporting more reliable quantitative analyses, especially for complex samples.
proposals.novelty: While chemometrics is applied in analytical chemistry, its systematic integration to model the intrinsic physicochemical properties of SPME coatings (binder-coating interactions) as direct predictors of displacement behavior represents a novel, mechanistic approach to understanding and controlling SPME processes.

proposals.title: Development of a Membrane-Integrated Solid-Phase Microextraction Fiber for the Selective Preconcentration and Analysis of Phenols in Complex Aqueous Matrices
proposals.motivation: Phenolic compounds are priority pollutants with significant environmental and health impacts, but their trace-level analysis in complex samples like wastewater and biological fluids remains challenging due to matrix interferences. Conventional SPME fibers, while excellent for preconcentration, can suffer from competitive adsorption and reduced selectivity for polar phenols. Integrating the selective permeability principles of membrane introduction mass spectrometry (MIMS) with SPME offers a promising route to enhance selectivity prior to analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: By fabricating an SPME fiber with a porous polymer membrane coating that acts as a selective barrier and preconcentration layer, we hypothesize that it will significantly improve the selectivity and sensitivity for target phenols by excluding larger matrix macromolecules and interfering compounds based on size and polarity.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel fiber by first coating a fused-silica core with a porous polypropylene or polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) membrane layer, using a controlled dip-coating or solvent casting method with a phase-inversion process to induce porosity. This membrane-coated fiber will then be further functionalized with a secondary, thin SPME extraction phase (e.g., a hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced (HLB) or C18 binder) to trap the permeated phenols. The extraction performance will be systematically evaluated using direct immersion SPME coupled to liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), comparing extraction efficiency, selectivity against humic acids and proteins, and kinetics to conventional coated fibers.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new class of SPME devices that combine membrane filtration with chemical extraction, directly addressing displacement effects in complex matrices. It will provide a validated, robust method for the accurate determination of free concentrations of phenols, expanding the quantitative application of SPME in environmental and bioanalytical chemistry.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of a size-exclusion and polarity-selective membrane layer as an integral part of the SPME fiber architecture, a concept adapted from MIMS but not previously applied to create a standalone, membrane-enhanced SPME device for phenolic compounds.

proposals.title: Parallelized, High-Throughput Analysis of Phenolic Compounds in Biological Fluids Using a 96-Well Plate-Compatible Solid-Phase Microextraction Array
proposals.motivation: The quantitative analysis of phenolic compounds (e.g., endocrine disruptors, polyphenols) in biological and environmental samples is crucial for exposure assessment and metabolomic studies. Current SPME methods, while effective for in vivo and non-depletive analysis, are inherently low-throughput, limiting their application in large-scale epidemiological or high-throughput screening studies. Scaling SPME to a 96-well plate format would bridge the gap between its unique advantages and the throughput demands of modern bioanalytical laboratories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by miniaturizing SPME fibers into a dense, arrayed format compatible with a 96-well plate geometry and employing sequential SPME principles with biocompatible coatings, we can achieve rapid, parallel extraction of phenols from complex matrices without significant displacement effects, enabling accurate high-throughput quantification.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a custom SPME array holder with 96 precisely aligned, shortened SPME fibers coated with a biocompatible, mixed-mode sorbent (e.g., C18/benzenesulfonic acid) to target a broad range of phenols. The array will be immersed into a 96-well plate containing pre-conditioned biological samples (e.g., plasma, urine). Extraction kinetics and equilibrium will be optimized for the miniaturized geometry. Following extraction, the entire array will be interfaced directly to a mass spectrometer via a multiplexed, improved microfluidic open interface (MOI) for automated, sequential thermal desorption and analysis, leveraging recent advances in Pawliszyn's group for direct SPME-MS coupling.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, ready-to-use protocol for parallel SPME of 96 samples simultaneously, drastically reducing analysis time per sample. It will provide new data on the kinetics and displacement behavior of phenolic compounds in ultra-high-throughput SPME formats. The developed platform will be a transformative tool for applications requiring rapid, green, and non-exhaustive sample preparation, such as clinical biomarker discovery and large-scale environmental monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While 96-well formats exist for other microextraction techniques, a true parallelized SPME array for direct, high-throughput MS analysis is novel. The integration of sequential SPME concepts to control displacement in a massively parallel setup, combined with direct coupling to MS via the MOI, represents a significant and non-obvious advancement beyond simple miniaturization of existing SPME automation.

proposals.title: Direct Coupling of In-Needle Nanomaterial Sorbent Phases to Mass Spectrometry via a Microfluidic Open Interface for Automated, Single-Step Extraction and Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current analytical workflows require separate, often manual, sample preparation and analysis steps, limiting throughput and introducing potential analyte loss or contamination. While Pawliszyn's group has pioneered direct SPME-MS coupling via the microfluidic open interface (MOI), the sorbent phase remains physically distinct from the detector. Fully integrating the extraction medium into the analytical flow path would represent the ultimate simplification, enabling true one-step, automated determinations. This is critical for applications like real-time environmental monitoring or high-throughput clinical screening where speed and automation are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: That a solid-phase microextraction device, where the extraction phase is composed of a structured nanomaterial sorbent and is physically integrated as a permanent, renewable component of a microfluidic mass spectrometry interface, can perform quantitative analysis without discrete, offline sample preparation steps.
proposals.method: We will fabricate SPME probes where the fused-silica fiber is replaced by a microfluidic channel packed with a high-surface-area nanomaterial sorbent (e.g., metal-organic frameworks or graphene oxide) bound with a biocompatible polymer. This 'extraction needle' will be directly coupled as the injection loop of an improved MOI. The method will involve a sequential, automated protocol: 1) sample loading/equilibration, 2) washing with a weak solvent to remove matrix interferences, and 3) quantitative desorption/elution directly into the MS ion source using a strong, MS-compatible solvent. The system's software will control all fluidics and timing, synchronizing extraction and analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully integrated analytical platform that eliminates the boundary between sample preparation and instrumental analysis, dramatically increasing throughput and automation. It will provide a foundational methodology for quantitative, single-step analysis of complex samples, directly advancing the fields of green analytical chemistry and ambient mass spectrometry. The developed protocols will be validated for determining free concentrations of challenging analytes, such as endocannabinoids in biofluids.
proposals.novelty: While direct SPME-MS exists, the permanent integration of a renewable, high-efficiency nanomaterial sorbent phase *within* the microfluidic interface itself, creating a unified 'extraction-detection' module, is a novel architecture. This moves beyond coupling two devices to fundamentally re-engineering the sample introduction system of the mass spectrometer to perform automated extraction.

proposals.title: Rational Design of Metal-Organic Framework/Polydopamine Composite Coatings for Solid-Phase Microextraction of Neuropeptides
proposals.motivation: The quantitative analysis of neuropeptides in complex biological matrices remains a significant challenge due to low endogenous concentrations, high sample complexity, and strong matrix effects. Existing SPME coatings lack the necessary selectivity and capacity for this specific analyte class, often leading to displacement effects and poor reproducibility in quantitative workflows. Addressing this gap is critical for advancing neurochemical research and biomarker discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a rationally designed composite SPME coating, integrating a metal-organic framework (MOF) with a biocompatible polydopamine (PDA) binder, will provide enhanced selectivity and capacity for neuropeptides through synergistic size-exclusion, hydrophilic interaction, and metal-affinity mechanisms, thereby improving quantitative accuracy by mitigating displacement.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of hydrophilic MOFs (e.g., ZIF-90, MIL-101(Cr)-NH2) with pore apertures tuned to the molecular size range of target neuropeptides. These MOF particles will be immobilized onto etched silica fibers using a controlled polydopamine deposition process, which acts as a robust, biocompatible adhesive and secondary interactive phase. The synthesis parameters (MOF: PDA ratio, deposition time) will be systematically optimized using a design-of-experiments (DoE) approach. Coating performance will be evaluated for a model mixture of neuropeptides (e.g., substance P, neurotensin) using direct coupling to nanoLC-MS/MS, assessing extraction efficiency, selectivity over proteins, and resistance to displacement.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new class of SPME coatings with rationally designed selectivity for peptides, directly addressing the displacement problem highlighted in recent Pawliszyn group research. It will establish a generalizable framework for tailoring MOF-PDA composites to specific analyte classes. The resulting high-performance coatings will enable more reliable determination of free neuropeptide concentrations in brain tissue and other biological samples.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate integration of a size-selective MOF with a multifunctional PDA binder to create a synergistic extraction phase specifically for peptides, moving beyond generic hydrophobic coatings. This approach directly applies the group's recent focus on biocompatible binders and displacement mitigation to the unsolved problem of peptide-selective extraction.

proposals.title: Fully Automated High-Throughput Analysis via a Robotic Platform Integrating Nanomaterial-Coated SPME Fibers and Direct-to-MS Interfacing
proposals.motivation: The integration of advanced nanomaterials into SPME coatings has demonstrated superior extraction efficiency and selectivity for complex samples. However, translating these laboratory-scale advancements into routine, high-throughput analysis is hindered by a lack of fully automated systems capable of handling the sequential conditioning, extraction, desorption, and cleaning of these sensitive devices without manual intervention, which introduces variability and limits throughput.
proposals.hypothesis: That a fully automated robotic analytical platform, integrating durable nanomaterial-based SPME devices and a microfluidic open interface (MOI) to mass spectrometry, can provide reproducible, high-throughput quantitative analysis of complex biological samples by systematically controlling all phases of the SPME workflow and mitigating displacement effects through sequential extraction protocols.
proposals.method: The project will develop a robotic autosampler platform equipped with a multi-fiber carousel. It will utilize SPME fibers coated with novel, robust biocompatible sorbents incorporating metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) or graphene oxide, immobilized with a polymeric binder. The system's software will automate a sequential SPME protocol: fiber conditioning in a dedicated port, immersion or headspace extraction from multi-well plates, desorption into a MOI coupled to an electrospray ionization mass spectrometer, and a cleaning cycle. The microfluidic interface will be optimized based on pre-2026 MOI designs to ensure efficient, carryover-free transfer of analytes from the nanomaterial coatings.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, automated platform that bridges the gap between advanced nanomaterial SPME coatings and practical, high-throughput quantitative analysis. It will provide a standardized method for the determination of free concentrations in complex matrices like tissue homogenates, significantly improving reproducibility and sample throughput compared to manual SPME operations.
proposals.novelty: While automated SPME exists, the full integration of next-generation nanomaterial coatings into a completely hands-free system that manages the entire lifecycle of the fiber is novel. The combination of automated sequential extraction protocols, designed to address displacement, with direct MS interfacing in a single platform represents a significant advance in operationalizing complex SPME methodologies.

proposals.title: A Nanofabricated Solid-Phase Microextraction Probe with Biocompatible Nanomaterial Coating for In Situ Metabolomic Profiling of Single Cells
proposals.motivation: Single-cell metabolomics is critical for understanding cellular heterogeneity in development, neuroscience, and cancer, but current methods struggle with the efficient, non-destructive extraction of low-abundance metabolites from nanoliter volumes. Pre-2026 SPME techniques, while excellent for in vivo and complex matrix analysis, are not miniaturized or sensitive enough for the spatial and volumetric constraints of individual mammalian cells. Bridging this gap would enable direct, quantitative measurements of intracellular metabolite dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: That a miniaturized SPME probe, fabricated via focused ion beam milling and coated with a tailored, biocompatible nanostructured extraction phase, can efficiently extract and pre-concentrate metabolites from single cells with minimal disruption, enabling their subsequent identification by nanoelectrospray mass spectrometry.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ultra-fine SPME probes (tip diameter < 1 µm) from fused silica fibers using focused ion beam (FIB) milling. A biocompatible polymeric binder (e.g., polyacrylonitrile or C18-polyacrylonitrile composite) will be used to immobilize tailored nanomaterials (e.g., metal-organic frameworks like ZIF-8 or functionalized graphene oxide) as the extraction phase. The probe will be integrated with a microfluidic open interface (MOI) coupled to a high-resolution mass spectrometer. A sequential SPME protocol will be developed to first extract hydrophilic metabolites and then lipophilic species, addressing displacement and ensuring comprehensive coverage.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, minimally invasive tool for the spatially resolved chemical analysis of single living cells, directly extending the principles of in vivo SPME to the sub-cellular scale. It will provide a methodological framework for quantifying the free concentration of metabolites within individual cells, advancing fundamental studies of metabolic heterogeneity. The integration of nanomaterial coatings with micro-fabricated supports represents a significant technical advancement in microextraction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the extreme miniaturization of the SPME support via FIB milling and the application of sequential, biocompatible nanomaterial coatings specifically designed for the complex, minute chemical milieu of a single cell, moving beyond tissue-level or bulk cell population analyses.

proposals.title: Integrated SPME-SERS Probes: Development of Binder-Free, Coated Fibers with Plasmonic Nanostructures for Quantitative In-Situ Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current analytical workflows for complex samples, such as brain tissue or environmental matrices, often require separate steps for extraction/pre-concentration and detection, limiting throughput and risking analyte loss. Solid-phase microextraction (SPME) excels at non-exhaustive sampling and determining free concentrations, but its coupling to detection typically requires desorption and separate instrumental analysis. Developing materials that combine efficient extraction with direct, on-probe signal transduction would streamline analysis and enable new in-situ measurement capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by synthesizing and immobilizing tailored plasmonic nanostructures (e.g., gold nanorods or nanostars) within a biocompatible, high-affinity SPME coating phase, we can create a dual-function sorbent that efficiently extracts target analytes and simultaneously acts as a substrate for their quantitative detection via surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS).
proposals.method: We will fabricate SPME fibers by first coating fused-silica supports with a thin, adherent layer of a biocompatible polymer (e.g., polyacrylonitrile or a C18-modified sol-gel) acting as a binder and extraction phase. Plasmonic nanoparticles will be synthesized with controlled morphology and embedded within this coating during its deposition. The extraction kinetics and capacity of these probes will be characterized using established SPME models. Following extraction, the same fiber will be interfaced with a Raman microscope; the embedded nanoparticles will provide the electromagnetic enhancement for SERS detection of the adsorbed analytes, eliminating the need for a separate desorption step.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, integrated sample preparation and detection platform that merges the quantitative, non-exhaustive nature of SPME with the molecular specificity and sensitivity of SERS. It will advance the field of microextraction by creating self-contained analytical probes, potentially enabling rapid, in-situ measurements in complex biological and environmental systems without extensive sample workup.
proposals.novelty: While SPME coatings and SERS substrates have been developed separately, their integration into a single, binder-stabilized material where the extraction phase is intrinsically the detection substrate is non-obvious. This approach moves beyond simply using a SPME fiber to transfer analytes to a separate SERS plate, aiming instead for a truly unified extraction-detection interface.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Screening of Biofluids Using a Parallelized Array of Nanomaterial-Coated Solid-Phase Microextraction Microtips Coupled to Automated MS Analysis
proposals.motivation: High-throughput screening in metabolomics and pharmacokinetics is bottlenecked by slow, sequential sample preparation. While Pawliszyn's group has advanced direct SPME-MS coupling via the microfluidic open interface (MOI), throughput remains limited by single-fiber analysis. A parallelized extraction platform is urgently needed to match the speed of modern mass spectrometers and enable large-scale clinical or environmental studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fabricating a dense array of identical, nanomaterial-coated SPME microtips will enable the simultaneous, quantitative extraction of analytes from multiple samples or sample replicates, dramatically increasing throughput without compromising the sensitivity or quantitative accuracy afforded by biocompatible coatings.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a 96-microtip array aligned with a standard microplate format using etched metal wires. Each tip will be coated via dip-coating with a uniform, biocompatible polymeric binder (e.g., polyacrylonitrile) embedded with tailored carbon nanotubes or metal-organic frameworks to enhance extraction efficiency. The array will be mounted on an automated robotic platform for simultaneous immersion into a 96-well plate. After extraction, the entire array will be interfaced directly to a high-resolution mass spectrometer via a custom, multi-channel version of the MOI, using a rapid thermal desorption and solvent elution protocol to sequentially analyze each microtip.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first truly parallel SPME-MS platform, reducing sample preparation time per sample from minutes to seconds. It will establish a validated protocol for high-throughput, quantitative analysis of small molecules in complex matrices, directly addressing the demand for rapid screening in precision medicine and exposomics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a high-density, nanomaterial-coated SPME array with direct MS analysis, moving beyond sequential single-fiber automation to genuine parallel extraction and desorption, a significant leap in SPME technology not yet demonstrated in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Development of a Multi-Fiber SPME Array with Orthogonal Coatings for High-Throughput, Multi-Analyte Sampling and Quantitative Analysis
proposals.motivation: Conventional single-fiber SPME, while powerful, is inherently limited to sequential sampling and often struggles with complex mixtures due to displacement effects and coating selectivity. For high-throughput applications in metabolomics, environmental monitoring, and pharmacokinetics, there is a critical need for devices that can simultaneously capture a broad range of analytes with varying physicochemical properties from a single sample, preserving the true chemical profile and reducing analysis time.
proposals.hypothesis: A precisely engineered array of SPME fibers, each functionalized with a distinct, biocompatible coating material (e.g., hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced, C18, mixed-mode, and restricted access materials), will enable the parallel, non-depletive extraction of multiple analyte classes from a single sample, mitigating displacement and providing a more comprehensive and quantitative snapshot of the sample's free concentration.
proposals.method: The project will fabricate a miniaturized, robust SPME array holder compatible with autosampler systems. Individual fibers will be coated with distinct, pre-2026 established phases (e.g., polyacrylonitrile, C18/benzenesulfonic acid mixed-mode, and novel biocompatible polymers from recent Pawliszyn group work) using established coating methods. The array will be coupled directly to a mass spectrometer via a microfluidic open interface (MOI) for high-throughput desorption and analysis. A key methodological focus will be developing a calibration and data analysis workflow that integrates extraction data from all fibers to generate a unified, quantitative multi-analyte profile, addressing fiber-to-fiber variability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, practical SPME device that significantly increases sample throughput and analytical coverage for complex samples. It will establish a foundational framework for parallel, multi-phasic microextraction, directly advancing quantitative analysis in systems biology and environmental science by providing a more holistic view of analyte distributions while minimizing sample perturbation.
proposals.novelty: While multi-fiber devices exist for sensitivity enhancement, this proposal inverts the paradigm by using an array of *orthogonal* coatings for parallel multi-analyte profiling from a single sampling event, integrated with a direct-to-MS high-throughput interface. This moves beyond using multiple fibers for a single purpose to using them as a unified, information-rich sampling tool.

proposals.title: Quantitative Analysis via Time-Controlled, Non-Equilibrium Solid-Phase Microextraction: A Kinetic Modeling Framework for Complex Matrices
proposals.motivation: Quantitative SPME analysis traditionally relies on achieving equilibrium or exhaustive extraction, which is time-consuming and often impractical for high-throughput analysis or in complex, dynamic systems like living tissues or process streams. This equilibrium requirement limits the application speed and can be invalidated by displacement effects in multi-analyte systems, a known challenge in the researcher's recent work. A robust kinetic model for short, non-equilibrium extractions would dramatically expand SPME's utility for rapid, in vivo, and online monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: That a universal kinetic model, correlating extracted mass solely with sampling time, diffusion coefficients, and coating geometry under well-defined agitation, can be developed to enable accurate quantification without requiring equilibrium, even in the presence of competitive binding and displacement effects.
proposals.method: We will develop and validate a computational framework based on Fick's laws of diffusion, explicitly solving the time-dependent concentration profile for analytes moving from a bulk sample matrix into a defined SPME coating. The model will be parameterized using experimentally determined diffusion coefficients (from literature and bespoke experiments) and coating dimensions (e.g., thickness of new biocompatible or binder-based coatings from recent papers). Calibration will involve a series of timed extractions under controlled agitation (e.g., using a standard stir rate or flow cell), with the model fitted to the kinetic uptake curves. Validation will be performed in complex matrices (e.g., brain homogenate, plasma) using the direct-MS microfluidic interface for high-throughput data acquisition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated theoretical and practical framework for performing fast, quantitative SPME where equilibrium is not feasible. It will directly enable more rapid high-throughput determinations and more accurate in vivo monitoring of free concentrations, as displacement effects can be accounted for kinetically. The outcome will be a major shift in SPME protocol design, from equilibrium-seeking to time-optimized kinetic extraction.
proposals.novelty: While kinetic calibration exists, this proposal integrates a first-principles diffusion model specifically with modern, high-throughput direct-MS interfaces and advanced biocompatible coatings to create a universally applicable kinetic quantification method, moving beyond empirical calibration or the need for equilibrium.

proposals.title: A Modular, Principles-Based Curriculum Framework for Teaching Solid-Phase Microextraction and Equilibrium Sampling in Analytical Chemistry
proposals.motivation: Despite SPME's widespread adoption in research and industry as a green, solvent-minimizing sample preparation technique, its fundamental principles of equilibrium extraction, coating chemistry, and the distinction between total and free concentration are often superficially covered in standard analytical chemistry curricula. This gap leads to suboptimal application and data misinterpretation by new practitioners, hindering the technique's full potential in fields like environmental monitoring, bioanalysis, and food safety.
proposals.hypothesis: That a structured, modular curriculum grounded in the core thermodynamic and kinetic principles of SPME, integrated with hands-on experiments using modern coatings and interfaces, will significantly improve students' conceptual understanding and practical competency compared to traditional, descriptive teaching methods.
proposals.method: The project will develop a five-module curriculum: 1) Fundamentals of equilibrium microextraction and coating-phase selection (e.g., PDMS, C18, mixed-mode); 2) Kinetics of extraction and the role of agitation; 3) Quantification approaches (external calibration, internal standard, and equilibrium sampling via coating-to-sample phase ratio); 4) Advanced applications, including sequential SPME to address displacement and direct coupling to MS via microfluidic open interfaces; 5) Method validation for complex samples. Each module combines interactive lectures, simulation exercises for kinetics and calibration, and lab experiments using biocompatible coatings and real-world samples (e.g., measuring free drug concentrations in simulated biological fluids).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive, freely available educational package (syllabi, lecture notes, lab manuals, and data sets) to standardize and elevate SPME teaching globally. It will bridge the gap between cutting-edge research in the Pawliszyn group—such as biocompatible coatings and high-throughput MS coupling—and foundational analytical chemistry education, training a new generation of scientists in rigorous, principles-based microextraction science.
proposals.novelty: While SPME is taught in some courses, this proposal systematically integrates recent methodological advances (e.g., addressing displacement with sequential SPME, direct MS coupling) and the critical concept of free concentration determination into a cohesive, principles-first curriculum, moving beyond a simple technique demonstration to deep conceptual mastery.

proposals.title: Development of a Sequential Multi-Phase SPME Platform with Biocompatible Binders for Minimizing Displacement and Matrix Effects in In Vivo Metabolomic Profiling
proposals.motivation: In vivo solid-phase microextraction (SPME) is a powerful tool for minimally invasive sampling in living organisms, but its quantitative accuracy in complex biological matrices is compromised by displacement effects and competitive binding from high-abundance compounds. This limits reliable characterization of low-concentration metabolites and biomarkers critical for understanding dynamic physiological processes. Pre-2026 work has identified displacement as a key challenge, yet a comprehensive solution for complex, in vivo systems is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequential SPME protocol employing a series of fiber coatings with tailored selectivity and biocompatible polymeric binders will significantly reduce displacement effects, enabling more accurate and reproducible determination of free concentrations for a broad range of analytes in living tissues.
proposals.method: The project will design and fabricate SPME fibers with a sequential coating architecture: an initial hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced (HLB) phase to capture high-abundance, potentially interfering compounds, followed by a specialized extraction phase (e.g., mixed-mode or restricted access material) for target analytes, all bound using novel, inert, and biocompatible polymeric binders. These fibers will be evaluated in vivo using a rodent model, with direct coupling to mass spectrometry via a microfluidic open interface (MOI) for high-throughput analysis. Extraction kinetics and displacement will be quantitatively modeled using kinetic calibration methods (e.g., on-fiber standardization) to validate the protocol's performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, optimized SPME protocol specifically designed for the quantitative challenges of in vivo analysis. It will provide a methodological framework to deconvolute matrix effects, advancing SPME from a primarily qualitative tool to a robust quantitative platform for in vivo metabolomics and pharmacokinetic studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the intentional, sequential multi-phase coating design aimed at pre-emptively managing displacement, combined with the application of next-generation biocompatible binders, moving beyond single-coating optimization to an integrated system solution for in vivo complexity.

proposals.title: Development of a High-Capacity, Biocompatible Solid-Phase Mesh Extraction (SPME) Device for Continuous, Preparative-Scale Environmental Monitoring of Organic Micropollutants
proposals.motivation: Current SPME fibers are limited in extraction capacity and surface area, making them unsuitable for continuous, high-volume sampling in environmental monitoring or for preparative-scale isolation of analytes from large sample volumes. The need exists for passive, in-situ sampling devices that can integrate contaminant concentrations over extended periods (e.g., weeks) in water bodies, providing time-weighted average concentrations without frequent manual intervention. This capability is critical for accurate risk assessment of emerging contaminants like pharmaceuticals and pesticides.
proposals.hypothesis: By fabricating a large-surface-area, three-dimensional SPME device using a porous, inert metal mesh substrate coated with a robust, biocompatible extraction phase, we can achieve significantly higher extraction capacity and kinetic uptake, enabling continuous, long-term environmental sampling and the collection of preparative quantities of analytes for definitive identification.
proposals.method: We will fabricate extraction devices from stainless steel or titanium mesh, creating a high-surface-area, flow-through geometry. A durable, biocompatible coating—such as a crosslinked polyacrylonitrile (PAN) or polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) binder mixed with divinylbenzene or C18 particles—will be immobilized onto the mesh via in-situ polymerization or dip-coating, informed by recent work on biocompatible SPME coatings. The device will be housed in a protective, permeable enclosure for field deployment. Performance will be evaluated in flow-through systems using standard mixtures of environmental pollutants, comparing extraction kinetics and capacity to commercial SPME fibers. Coupling to liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) will be used for quantitative analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new class of SPME-based sampling devices capable of long-term, unattended environmental monitoring, bridging the gap between analytical microextraction and preparative-scale sampling. It will provide a practical tool for obtaining more representative contaminant data in aquatic systems and enable the collection of sufficient analyte mass for subsequent toxicological or confirmatory analysis using techniques like NMR.
proposals.novelty: While SPME coatings and geometries have been optimized for analytical sensitivity, scaling the physical format to a high-capacity, robust mesh device for continuous environmental deployment is a novel application. The integration of durable, biocompatible phase chemistry with a preparative-scale, field-ready hardware design moves beyond the lab-centric use of SPME.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Perturbation: A Combined Experimental and Finite Element Modeling Approach to Analyte Depletion in Solid-Phase Microextraction
proposals.motivation: A core assumption of equilibrium SPME is negligible depletion, ensuring the measured concentration reflects the original sample. However, in applications with small sample volumes, high fiber capacity, or complex matrices like brain tissue, this assumption may be violated, leading to quantitative inaccuracies. Understanding and modeling this depletion is critical for validating SPME's use in quantitative analysis, especially for in vivo and microscale sampling where sample integrity is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that analyte depletion during SPME can be systematically modeled using diffusion-reaction principles, and that the degree of depletion is a predictable function of fiber coating properties, sample volume, and analyte binding constants in the sample matrix.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual experimental and computational approach. Experimentally, we will use sequential SPME with thin, biocompatible coatings (e.g., C18-PAN, mixed-mode) in well-defined, small-volume samples containing model analytes with varying affinities for a binder protein (e.g., HSA). The concentration profile will be tracked over time. Computationally, we will develop a 2D axisymmetric finite element model in COMSOL Multiphysics, simulating the transient diffusion of analyte from the sample bulk and binding sites to the fiber coating, using experimentally determined partition coefficients and binding constants as inputs to predict the spatial and temporal concentration gradient.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated predictive framework for estimating concentration perturbation during SPME, moving beyond the rule-of-thumb 'negligible depletion' criterion. It will yield practical guidelines for coating selection and sampling time to minimize error in challenging matrices, directly strengthening the theoretical foundation for quantitative SPME, particularly in bioanalysis and environmental microsampling.
proposals.novelty: While depletion is acknowledged, a predictive, spatially-resolved model coupling fiber extraction kinetics with competitive binding in the sample is lacking. This proposal uniquely integrates Pawliszyn's recent work on sequential SPME and biocompatible coatings with rigorous computational fluid dynamics modeling to solve this 'dual problem'.

proposals.title: Direct Coupling of Biocompatible SPME to LC-MS/MS for the Quantitative Analysis of Semi-Volatile Compounds: Overcoming Matrix Effects and Displacement in Complex Samples
proposals.motivation: Gas chromatography (GC) has been the default separation technique for semi-volatile analytes coupled with SPME, largely due to thermal desorption compatibility. However, many semi-volatile compounds, such as pharmaceuticals, modern pesticides, and larger endogenous metabolites, are thermally labile or insufficiently volatile, making LC-MS a potentially superior platform. The challenge lies in adapting the direct SPME-MS workflow, successfully demonstrated for GC-amenable analytes, to the liquid-phase desorption and separation environment of LC-MS while maintaining quantitative rigor.
proposals.hypothesis: That a microfluidic open interface (MOI) optimized for LC mobile phase flow, coupled with a sequential SPME protocol using advanced biocompatible coatings, can provide robust, quantitative analysis of semi-volatile compounds by LC-MS/MS, outperforming GC-based methods in terms of analyte scope and mitigating displacement effects in complex matrices.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a next-generation MOI with a low-dead-volume desorption chamber and integrated switching valve to interface SPME fibers directly with an LC-MS/MS system. Biocompatible mixed-mode coatings (e.g., C18/SCX particles in a polyacrylonitrile binder) will be used for sequential extraction of model semi-volatile analytes (e.g., specific pharmaceuticals, endocannabinoids). The protocol will involve an initial short desorption (to release loosely bound, displacement-prone analytes) followed by a full desorption, with both fractions analyzed. Quantification will employ kinetic calibration models and stable isotope-labeled standards to account for matrix effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a validated, high-throughput direct SPME-LC-MS/MS workflow, expanding the application of SPME to a broader class of thermally sensitive semi-volatiles. It will provide a fundamental comparison of LC vs. GC separation in the context of SPME, delivering a practical framework for quantitative analysis that explicitly addresses displacement and matrix effects in biological and environmental samples.
proposals.novelty: While direct SPME-MS interfaces exist, their application has been predominantly for GC separations or direct infusion. This proposal innovates by specifically engineering the interface and protocol for seamless LC coupling and by leveraging sequential extraction with biocompatible coatings to solve quantification challenges unique to the LC analysis of complex samples.

proposals.title: Direct High-Sensitivity Analysis of Complex Samples via Ultra-Low Dispersion Flow Injection Coupled to Trapped Ion Mobility Spectrometry-Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: The paradigm in analytical chemistry, particularly for complex biological and environmental matrices, assumes that a sample preparation or pre-concentration step like SPME is mandatory to achieve sufficient sensitivity and reduce matrix effects. This adds time, cost, and complexity, limiting high-throughput applications. Recent advances in ultra-sensitive mass spectrometry and low-dispersion fluidics challenge this necessity, offering a path to radical simplification.
