proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating ultra-low dispersion direct injection with advanced ion mobility separation and state-of-the-art high-resolution mass spectrometry, the sensitivity and selectivity required for quantitative analysis of low-abundance analytes in complex samples can be achieved without any prior extraction or pre-concentration.
proposals.method: We will develop a dedicated microfluidic flow injection system with minimized tubing dimensions and zero-dead-volume connections to deliver sub-microliter sample plugs directly to the detector with minimal band broadening. This will be coupled to a trapped ion mobility spectrometry-quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometer (TIMS-QTOF MS). The TIMS cell will provide gas-phase separation and clean-up, isolating analyte ions from matrix isobars. Specific data processing algorithms, including machine learning-based feature detection and differential mobility filtering, will be employed to deconvolute signals in the total ion chromatogram.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for direct-injection analytical protocols, challenging the entrenched need for pre-concentration. It will provide a benchmark for the sensitivity limits achievable without extraction, potentially revolutionizing workflows for high-throughput screening in metabolomics and environmental monitoring. The methodology will quantify the trade-offs between sensitivity, speed, and matrix tolerance.
proposals.novelty: While direct injection exists for clean samples, applying it quantitatively to undiluted, complex biological tissues (e.g., brain homogenate) without any sample workup is non-obvious. The novelty lies in the systematic coupling of cutting-edge, low-dispersion fluidics with TIMS for in-situ gas-phase matrix separation, a concept not fully explored for bypassing liquid-phase microextraction entirely.

proposals.title: Solvent-Free, High-Throughput Quantitative Analysis via Sequential SPME with Biocompatible, High-Capacity Binder Phases Coupled Directly to MS
proposals.motivation: Conventional analytical methods rely heavily on organic solvents for extraction, generating significant hazardous waste and complicating the direct analysis of complex biological matrices like brain tissue. The drive for green chemistry and the need for rapid, high-throughput determinations in fields such as metabolomics and neurochemistry demand a paradigm shift away from solvent-intensive protocols. Solid-phase microextraction (SPME) offers a solvent-minimized path, but challenges remain in quantitative accuracy due to displacement effects and in interfacing seamlessly with mass spectrometry for rapid analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: That a sequential SPME approach, utilizing novel biocompatible coating materials with enhanced capacity and selectivity, can achieve complete, quantitative extraction of target analytes (e.g., endocannabinoids) without organic solvents, and that direct coupling to mass spectrometry via a refined microfluidic open interface will enable accurate, high-throughput determinations.
proposals.method: The project will develop new SPME fiber coatings by embedding high-affinity sorbents (e.g., modified porous polymers or carbon-based materials) within a biocompatible polymeric binder (e.g., polyacrylonitrile or C18-polyacrylonitrile blends) to maximize phase loading and mitigate displacement. These fibers will be deployed in a sequential extraction protocol for complex samples. The extracted analytes will be directly desorbed and introduced into a mass spectrometer using an improved microfluidic open interface (MOI), optimizing flow rates and geometry for maximum desorption efficiency and minimal carryover, enabling automated, high-throughput analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully validated, solvent-free analytical workflow for the quantitative determination of endogenous compounds in biological tissues. It will advance green analytical chemistry by eliminating a major source of toxic waste and demonstrate how SPME can move beyond equilibrium-based sampling to exhaustive extraction. The integrated system will set a new standard for rapid, direct, and environmentally friendly bioanalysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of a sequential extraction strategy using purpose-built, high-capacity biocompatible phases to overcome displacement, with a streamlined direct-to-MS interface designed for complete desorption and high throughput, moving beyond proof-of-concept to a robust, quantitative solvent-free method.

proposals.title: Quantitative Analysis of Trace Organic Contaminants in Complex Environmental Waters Using Sequential Solid-Phase Microextraction with Biocompatible Coatings Coupled to High-Throughput MS
proposals.motivation: The quantitative analysis of trace organic contaminants (e.g., pharmaceuticals, pesticides) in complex aqueous matrices like wastewater and seawater remains a significant challenge due to matrix effects, fouling, and displacement phenomena on extraction phases. Pre-2026, direct SPME-MS methods offer speed but require rigorous validation for such demanding samples to ensure accurate determination of free concentrations, which is critical for environmental risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequential SPME protocol employing biocompatible, hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced (HLB) or mixed-mode coatings will mitigate displacement and matrix fouling effects, enabling robust, quantitative determinations of multi-class contaminants in high-complexity environmental waters when coupled to a microfluidic open interface (MOI) for high-throughput MS.
proposals.method: The method will utilize sequential SPME with custom HLB/polyacrylate fibers to pre-extract matrix interferents, followed by extraction of target analytes. Performance will be validated for wastewater, river water, and seawater spiked with a suite of pharmaceuticals and pesticides. The optimized protocol will be directly coupled to a triple quadrupole MS via an improved MOI for automated, high-throughput analysis. Key validation parameters (linearity, LODs, accuracy, precision, matrix effects) will be assessed against traditional SPE-LC-MS/MS.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully validated, high-throughput SPME-MS method for complex environmental waters, directly addressing quantitative challenges posed by displacement and matrix effects. It will provide a practical framework for environmental laboratories to adopt direct SPME-MS for routine monitoring, advancing the field of green analytical chemistry by minimizing solvent use while increasing sample throughput.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic application and validation of the sequential SPME concept with biocompatible coatings specifically for the most challenging environmental water matrices, coupled directly to high-throughput MS, which has not been comprehensively reported pre-2026.

proposals.title: Systematic Optimization of Solvent-Bar Dispersive Liquid-Liquid Microextraction via a Design-of-Experiments and Coating Chemistry Approach for Sub-ng/mL Detection Limits in Environmental Water Analysis
proposals.motivation: The demand for ultra-trace analysis of emerging contaminants (e.g., pharmaceuticals, pesticides) in complex environmental matrices is critical for accurate risk assessment. Current SB-DLLME methods, while offering high enrichment, often lack systematic optimization for achieving consistent sub-ng/mL limits of detection (LODs) required by modern regulations. This gap limits the reliable monitoring of these pollutants at environmentally relevant concentrations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multivariate optimization of SB-DLLME parameters, coupled with the strategic use of biocompatible SPME coating materials as dispersant modifiers, will significantly enhance extraction efficiency and phase separation stability, thereby enabling robust sub-ng/mL LODs.
proposals.method: The project will employ a central composite design to systematically optimize critical SB-DLLME parameters: type and volume of extraction/disperser solvents, salt concentration, and extraction time. The key innovation is the integration of biocompatible SPME coating materials (e.g., C18 particles, hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced polymers) into the disperser solvent phase to act as a binder and nucleation aid, improving droplet coalescence and analyte partitioning. The optimized method will be validated using LC-MS/MS for a panel of representative polar and non-polar contaminants in surface water.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a rigorously optimized, high-performance SB-DLLME protocol capable of sub-ng/mL quantification, directly addressing needs in environmental monitoring. It will also establish a novel hybrid methodology that bridges the principles of SB-DLLME and SPME coating technology, expanding the toolbox for microextraction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate, systematic fusion of SB-DLLME with advanced SPME coating chemistry, using coatings not as the extraction phase but as dispersant modifiers to control microdroplet dynamics—a non-obvious synergy not explored in prior literature.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput, Quantitative Membrane Introduction Mass Spectrometry Platform Enabled by Sequential SPME Fiber Coatings for Real-Time Monitoring of Organic Micropollutants in Water
proposals.motivation: Continuous, real-time monitoring of organic micropollutants (e.g., pesticides, pharmaceuticals) in water streams is critical for environmental protection and water security. Current methods like grab sampling with offline analysis miss transient contamination events and dynamic concentration profiles. Membrane Introduction Mass Spectrometry (MIMS) offers direct, continuous analysis but struggles with sensitivity for less volatile analytes and quantitative accuracy due to matrix effects and membrane fouling.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a sequential, multi-fiber Solid-Phase Microextraction (SPME) sampling strategy with a MIMS interface will enable sensitive, quantitative, and high-throughput real-time analysis of semi-volatile organic micropollutants in complex water matrices by providing controlled pre-concentration and mitigating displacement/fouling effects.
proposals.method: We will develop a microfluidic open interface (MOI) to couple a novel, automated sequential SPME fiber exchanger to a MIMS system. Biocompatible SPME coatings (e.g., hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced particles in a stable binder) will be optimized for target pollutant classes. Fibers will be exposed to the water stream via a flow-through cell, then rapidly transferred to the MOI for thermal desorption and MS analysis. A sequential protocol, using multiple fibers in rotation, will allow continuous monitoring while one fiber is analyzing and others are loading, addressing kinetic limitations and displacement.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel hybrid MIMS-SPME platform for the direct, quantitative, and continuous monitoring of semi-volatile organics in water. It will advance the field of real-time environmental mass spectrometry by solving key sensitivity and fouling challenges. The methodology will provide a new paradigm for high-throughput, on-site water quality surveillance.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious integration of sequential SPME—a technique developed for addressing displacement in quantitative bioanalysis—with a MIMS flow system creates a new platform for continuous monitoring. This moves beyond using SPME as a batch sampling tool, transforming it into an integral, renewable component of a real-time sensor.

proposals.title: High-Throughput, In-Vivo SPME Monitoring of Phthalate Metabolites and Bisphenol A in Human Urine and Serum Using Sequential Biocompatible Fiber Extraction
proposals.motivation: Human exposure to endocrine-disrupting chemicals like phthalate esters (PAEs) and bisphenol A (BPA) is widespread and a significant public health concern, necessitating accurate biomonitoring. Current methods for analyzing their metabolites in complex biological fluids often require extensive sample preparation, lack high-throughput capability, and may not accurately reflect free, bioavailable concentrations. A robust, direct analysis technique is urgently needed for large-scale epidemiological studies.
proposals.hypothesis: That a sequential, two-fiber solid-phase microextraction (SPME) approach, utilizing biocompatible coatings to mitigate displacement effects, can provide rapid, accurate, and high-throughput determination of free concentrations of major PAE metabolites and BPA in urine and serum when directly coupled to liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS).
proposals.method: We will develop a method using two sequential SPME fibers with distinct, biocompatible coatings (e.g., hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced and mixed-mode materials) to comprehensively extract and pre-concentrate polar PAE metabolites and BPA from minimally processed urine and serum. The microextraction will be directly coupled to an LC-MS system via a refined microfluidic open interface (MOI) for automated, high-throughput analysis. The sequential protocol and coating chemistry will be optimized to quantitatively address analyte displacement and matrix binding effects, ensuring accurate free concentration measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, high-throughput SPME-LC-MS method for the simultaneous determination of key endocrine disruptor biomarkers, directly addressing the displacement challenge in quantitative bioanalysis. It will provide a practical tool for large-scale human biomonitoring studies, enabling more accurate assessment of exposure and associated health risks based on free, bioactive concentrations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the application of a sequential, dual-coating SPME protocol specifically designed to overcome displacement effects for these co-occurring biomarker classes in biological fluids, combined with direct high-throughput coupling to MS via a microfluidic interface, which is not established for this application.

proposals.title: Systematic Identification of Volatile and Semi-Volatile Analytes Where Static Headspace Outperforms Headspace Solid-Phase Microextraction: A Thermodynamic and Kinetic Profiling Study
proposals.motivation: Headspace solid-phase microextraction (HS-SPME) is often assumed to be universally superior to static headspace (S-HS) sampling due to its preconcentration capability. However, this assumption lacks rigorous, systematic validation and may lead to suboptimal method selection for specific compound classes. A definitive study identifying analytes where the simpler, equilibrium-based S-HS technique provides equal or better sensitivity and accuracy is needed to refine fundamental sampling theory and guide practical analytical workflows.
proposals.hypothesis: For specific classes of highly volatile, low-affinity, or competitively binding analytes, static headspace sampling will demonstrate superior analytical performance (e.g., accuracy, precision, and linear dynamic range) compared to HS-SPME due to the absence of coating-specific thermodynamic biases and kinetic limitations.
proposals.method: We will employ a comparative platform using a single GC-MS system with automated valve switching to analyze identical samples via S-HS and HS-SPME in parallel. A diverse test mixture will include alkanes, aldehydes, ketones, and small polar organics. HS-SPME will utilize coatings of varying polarity (PDMS, PDMS/DVB, CAR/PDMS) and thickness. Key metrics will be extracted via calibration curves: sensitivity (slope), limit of detection, linear range, and precision. Data analysis will involve calculating the phase ratio (β) for S-HS and the coating/gas distribution constant (Kfs) for SPME, with principal component analysis used to cluster analytes where S-HS performance metrics exceed those of HS-SPME.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clear, experimentally grounded decision framework for selecting headspace sampling methods, moving beyond heuristic assumptions. It will expand the fundamental understanding of SPME limitations, particularly for analytes prone to displacement or with unfavorable partitioning kinetics. The results will be compiled into a practical guideline for analytical chemists in environmental, food, and metabolomic studies.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies compare methods for specific applications, a systematic, thermodynamic-driven profiling to *categorically identify* analyte classes where static headspace is superior is novel. The approach directly challenges a prevailing assumption in the SPME field by leveraging the group's deep expertise in SPME fundamentals to validate the counterpoint.

proposals.title: In-Vivo Solid-Phase Microextraction for Real-Time, Non-Destructive Monitoring of Volatile Metabolite Kinetics During Yogurt Fermentation
proposals.motivation: Real-time, in-situ monitoring of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) during food fermentation is critical for process control and quality assurance, yet current methods often require destructive sampling or lack temporal resolution. Pre-2026, in-vivo SPME has been successfully applied in biomedical and environmental contexts, but its application to dynamic, complex food matrices like fermenting yogurt remains largely unexplored. This gap limits our understanding of the real-time metabolic interplay between starter cultures that defines product flavor and texture.
proposals.hypothesis: That biocompatible SPME coatings, deployed directly within a fermenting yogurt matrix, can provide continuous, quantitative profiles of key flavor and process-relevant VOCs (e.g., acetaldehyde, diacetyl, acetoin) without disrupting the microbial ecosystem or fermentation kinetics.
proposals.method: We will employ sequential SPME using fibers coated with biocompatible, hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced (HLB) particles in a polyacrylonitrile (PAN) binder, as recently developed in the Pawliszyn group to mitigate displacement effects. Multiple fibers will be inserted into a laboratory-scale fermenter and sequentially withdrawn at programmed intervals for direct thermal desorption and analysis via a GC-MS coupled to a microfluidic open interface (MOI) for high-throughput analysis. A key technical focus will be calibrating for the free concentration of VOCs in the complex, changing matrix using kinetic calibration or standard-free quantification approaches validated in pre-2026 SPME work.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel analytical framework for non-invasive, real-time metabolic monitoring in food fermentation, providing unprecedented kinetic data on flavor compound formation. It will extend the application domain of in-vivo SPME to controlled industrial bioprocesses and deliver a practical tool for optimizing fermentation endpoints and diagnosing process deviations based on volatile metabolite fingerprints.
proposals.novelty: While in-vivo SPME is known, its application for continuous, multi-point temporal profiling within a physically and chemically evolving dairy fermentation matrix is novel. The integration of sequential sampling with biocompatible coatings specifically designed to address displacement in complex media for quantitative VOC analysis in food has not been reported pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Combinatorial Materials Screening Platform for the Discovery of Application-Specific Solid-Phase Microextraction Coatings
proposals.motivation: Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) remains the default, 'universal' SPME coating despite known limitations in selectivity and sensitivity for many polar or complex analytes, a compromise rooted in historical precedent rather than systematic optimization. This reliance on a single material constrains analytical performance and limits SPME's potential for targeted applications, such as in vivo bioanalysis or environmental monitoring of emerging contaminants. A paradigm shift from a one-fibre-fits-all approach to a rational, application-driven coating selection is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-throughput combinatorial screening of a diverse library of coating materials, encompassing varied chemical functionalities and morphologies, will systematically identify coatings that significantly outperform PDMS for specific, representative analytical challenges.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a library of 50-100 distinct SPME fibres using automated dip-coating and sol-gel techniques on a multi-fibre array format. Coatings will include novel polymeric blends, molecularly imprinted polymers, metal-organic frameworks (e.g., ZIF-8, UiO-66), and advanced biocompatible phases like polyacrylonitrile and C18-bonded silica, building on the group's recent work with sequential SPME and biocompatible binders. Screening will be performed using a robotic autosampler coupled to a modified microfluidic open interface (MOI) for direct, high-throughput MS analysis, evaluating extraction efficiency and selectivity against a complex matrix such as brain homogenate or wastewater.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational methodology for the rapid discovery and optimization of SPME coatings, moving the field towards application-specific fibre design. It will produce a ranked database of coating performance for a model analyte set, providing a rational framework for future method development and challenging the entrenched status of PDMS.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in directly translating the high-throughput combinatorial discovery paradigm from heterogeneous catalysis to analytical sample preparation, a concept not previously applied to SPME. The integration of automated fibre fabrication with direct, parallelized MS screening via the MOI creates a uniquely powerful discovery platform.

proposals.title: Inverted Stable Isotope Dilution SPME with Matrix-Derived Internal Standardization for the Robust Quantification of Flavour Metabolites in Yoghurt
proposals.motivation: Accurate quantification of flavour compounds in complex, protein- and fat-rich matrices like yoghurt is challenged by severe matrix effects that compromise external calibration and the prohibitive cost of synthesizing isotopically labeled standards for every analyte. Current SPME methods, including direct coupling to MS, require robust calibration strategies to achieve true accuracy in food analysis, which is critical for quality control and flavour development.
proposals.hypothesis: That a single stable isotope-labeled standard can be used to first quantify its native analog via an inverted SIDA protocol in solvent, and that this quantified native compound will then serve as a perfectly matrix-matched internal standard to correct for matrix-induced signal variation for all co-extracted, structurally related flavour metabolites during SPME-MS analysis.
proposals.method: We will employ sequential SPME with a biocompatible coating (e.g., C18-PAN) to minimize displacement. First, a calibration curve will be built in pure solvent using a 13C-labeled key flavour compound (e.g., diacetyl). This curve will quantify the native compound in a yoghurt extract. Subsequently, this quantified native analyte will be used as a matrix-derived internal standard for the relative quantification of other flavour compounds (e.g., acetoin, 2,3-butanediol) via standard addition, applying correction factors determined from the solvent calibration. The microfluidic open interface (MOI) will be used for direct, high-throughput SPME-MS coupling.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, cost-effective calibration framework that extends the absolute accuracy of SIDA to an entire compound class within a single analysis. It will provide a validated SPME-MS protocol for matrix-robust quantification in dairy products, significantly advancing quantitative food metabolomics. The method synergistically combines the strengths of external calibration, SIDA, and internal standardization.
proposals.novelty: The inversion of the SIDA role for the labeled standard and the strategic repurposing of the quantified native analyte as a matrix-derived universal internal standard is a non-obvious fusion of established concepts, creating a new paradigm for calibration that has not been reported in SPME or food analysis literature pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Unified Framework for Analytical Performance: Validating the Work of Analytical Chemistry (WAC) Metrics Across Chromatography, Spectroscopy, and Chemical Sensor Platforms
proposals.motivation: The analytical chemistry community lacks a universal, quantitative framework to compare the fundamental performance of disparate techniques, from separation science to direct sensing. The emerging 'Work of Analytical Chemistry' (WAC) concept, which quantifies the minimum thermodynamic work required to obtain an analytical result, offers a potential unifying metric. Validating WAC across technique classes is crucial for objective method selection, optimization, and fostering a physics-based understanding of analytical limits.
proposals.hypothesis: The WAC metric can be robustly calculated and experimentally validated for a diverse set of analytical techniques, and it will provide a consistent, technique-agnostic measure of fundamental analytical efficiency that correlates with traditional figures of merit like limit of detection and resolution.
proposals.method: We will adapt the WAC calculation framework to three distinct domains: (1) chromatography (HPLC-UV), (2) spectroscopy (micro-Raman), and (3) solid-phase microextraction (SPME)-based chemical sensors. For SPME, a core Pawliszyn-group technology, we will calculate WAC for determinations using different coating phases (e.g., PDMS, C18, biocompatible binders) and coupling methods (e.g., direct-to-MS via a microfluidic open interface). Experimental validation will involve analyzing standardized mixtures (e.g., endocannabinoids, pharmaceuticals) and comparing WAC values to empirical performance data, using sequential SPME protocols to address displacement and validate free concentration measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first cross-platform experimental validation of WAC, establishing its practical utility. It will yield a standardized protocol for calculating WAC in different analytical contexts, enabling direct comparison of techniques. For the SPME field, it will offer a novel, fundamental metric for coating and interface design, moving beyond empirical optimization.
proposals.novelty: While WAC has been proposed theoretically, its systematic experimental application and validation across fundamentally different analytical techniques (separation, spectral, and equilibrium-based microextraction/sensing) is unprecedented. Applying this framework specifically to SPME methodologies, where the 'work' of extraction and desorption is central, represents a novel conceptual advancement for the field.

proposals.title: Automated, High-Throughput Quantitative SPME via Integrated Microfluidic Fiber Handling and Washing
proposals.motivation: Direct coupling of SPME fibers to mass spectrometry (MS) via microfluidic open interfaces (MOI) has enabled rapid analysis but remains limited by manual sample handling and fiber washing steps, creating a bottleneck for high-throughput applications. Automated, sequential SPME analysis of complex biological samples, such as brain tissue homogenates, is hindered by the need to address displacement effects and carryover between samples without operator intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating SPME fiber manipulation with a purpose-designed microfluidic chip for automated sample delivery, extraction, and sequential washing will enable reproducible, high-throughput quantitative analysis by minimizing carryover and standardizing the entire sample preparation workflow.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)/glass microfluidic chip with integrated channels and chambers to accept a commercial SPME fiber. The chip will interface with the existing MOI-MS system. Using programmable syringe pumps and valves, the chip will automate: (1) delivery of a discrete sample plug (e.g., tissue homogenate) to the fiber for extraction, (2) sequential washing with a series of biocompatible buffer solutions (informed by recent work on displacement effects) to desorb matrix components, and (3) final elution/desorption into the MOI for MS analysis. System control and timing will be managed via a LabVIEW interface.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully automated microfluidic-SPME-MS platform that significantly increases sample throughput and analytical reproducibility for complex matrices. It will provide a validated method for sequential analysis that actively manages displacement and carryover, advancing the quantitative accuracy of SPME for applications like in-vivo monitoring and high-throughput bioanalysis.
proposals.novelty: While SPME has been coupled to microfluidics for sample introduction, the novel integration of automated, sequential washing protocols within a dedicated chip for fiber handling represents a significant step towards a complete, hands-free SPME workflow, moving beyond simple coupling to true process automation.

proposals.title: Piezoelectric Vibration-Assisted Solid-Phase Microextraction: A Novel Approach to Accelerate Kinetics and Enhance Sensitivity for Bioanalytical Applications
proposals.motivation: Despite its widespread adoption, conventional SPME is often limited by slow extraction kinetics, particularly for high molecular weight or strongly matrix-bound analytes, which leads to long equilibrium times and reduced throughput. This is a critical bottleneck in fields like neurochemistry and pharmacokinetics, where rapid, sensitive measurement of free concentrations is essential. Enhancing the mass transfer of analytes from the sample matrix to the fiber coating without compromising the biocompatibility or quantitative accuracy of the method remains a significant challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying controlled, low-frequency piezoelectric vibration directly to the SPME fiber during immersion will significantly enhance the convective mass transfer of analytes to the coating, thereby reducing equilibrium time and increasing extraction efficiency, while maintaining the integrity of biocompatible coatings and preventing displacement effects.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a custom piezoelectric actuator integrated into a commercial SPME fiber holder to induce controlled, axial vibration (e.g., 10-100 Hz) of the coated fiber. The system will be tested using sequential SPME protocols with biocompatible coatings (e.g., C18-PAN, mixed-mode) to analyze complex biological samples such as brain homogenate for endocannabinoids. Extraction kinetics and efficiency with and without vibration will be rigorously compared using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS). Key parameters to optimize include vibration frequency, amplitude, and duration, correlating them with theoretical mass transfer models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, practical modality for kinetic enhancement in SPME, directly contributing to higher throughput and lower detection limits in bioanalysis. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how mechanical agitation at the fiber interface affects mass transfer and coating performance. The successful integration of vibration with sequential SPME protocols will offer a robust tool for more accurate quantitative analysis of free concentrations in complex matrices.
proposals.novelty: While bulk sample sonication is known, the direct, controlled mechanical vibration of the SPME fiber itself is a novel and unexplored approach to perturb the boundary layer and accelerate extraction. This method uniquely combines principles of piezoelectric actuation with the established framework of biocompatible SPME, targeting the kinetic limitation without altering the sample's chemical composition or the coating's extraction phase.

proposals.title: Inverted Solid-Phase Microextraction: A Novel Platform for Controlled Delivery and Passive Dosing of Bioactive Compounds in Aqueous Media
proposals.motivation: Precise control over the free concentration of bioactive compounds in aqueous matrices is critical for environmental toxicology, pharmaceutical release studies, and in vitro bioassays. Current passive dosing methods, such as polymer-loaded vials or generator columns, often lack spatiotemporal control and can be cumbersome for high-throughput applications. A simple, direct, and tunable delivery platform is needed to establish and maintain defined, stable aqueous concentrations for dynamic biological and chemical studies.
proposals.hypothesis: That SPME fiber coatings, traditionally used for extraction, can be repurposed as a controlled-release platform, where the partitioning thermodynamics and kinetics governing absorption can be inverted to govern desorption, enabling predictable and tunable delivery of target analytes into water.
proposals.method: We will fabricate SPME fibers with established biocompatible coatings (e.g., polyacrylate, C18, or mixed-mode materials) pre-loaded with target compounds (e.g., pharmaceuticals, endocannabinoid analogs). Using a microfluidic open interface coupled to a mass spectrometer, we will monitor in real-time the desorption kinetics and equilibrium concentrations in a flowing aqueous system. We will systematically vary coating chemistry, thickness, and flow conditions to develop a predictive model linking coating properties to release profiles, building on established kinetic and equilibrium models for SPME extraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational methodology for 'inverted SPME' as a novel passive dosing and delivery tool. It will provide a quantitative framework for using SPME fibers as programmable sources, not just sinks, for analytes. This will directly benefit fields requiring controlled aqueous exposure, such as ecotoxicology and drug release studies, by offering a high-throughput, fiber-based alternative.
proposals.novelty: While SPME is universally recognized as an extraction technique, its systematic application as a calibrated delivery device is unexplored. The novelty lies in fundamentally inverting the core function of the technology and leveraging the extensive pre-2026 knowledge of SPME coating-analyte interactions to predict and control reverse partitioning processes.

proposals.title: Development of Sub-Micron Biocompatible Solid-Phase Microextraction Fibers for In Vivo Spatial Metabolomics in Single Cells and Microfluidic Organ-on-a-Chip Devices
proposals.motivation: Current in-vivo SPME probes, while minimally invasive, are too large for sampling within single cells or the micro-channels of organ-on-a-chip devices, limiting spatial resolution and perturbing local microenvironments. A true micro-scale sampling tool is needed to map metabolic heterogeneity in complex biological systems and to perform longitudinal pharmacokinetic studies in microfluidic cell cultures without volume depletion.
proposals.hypothesis: By scaling down SPME fiber diameters to the sub-micron range and applying ultrathin, tailored biocompatible coatings, it is possible to perform direct, in-situ micro-sampling from single living cells and microfluidic channels with minimal disruption, enabling accurate determination of local free analyte concentrations.
proposals.method: We will fabricate sub-micron SPME fibers using etched silica capillaries or carbon filaments. A novel, sequential coating approach will be employed: first, a biocompatible polyacrylonitrile (PAN) or polyetheretherketone (PEEK) binder layer will be applied for durability, followed by a ultrathin (nanometer-scale) extraction phase (e.g., C18, mixed-mode, or hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced particles) optimized for small molecule metabolites. These micro-probes will be coupled to a microfluidic open interface (MOI) for direct, high-throughput infusion into a high-resolution mass spectrometer. Validation will involve sampling from microfluidic droplets containing known analytes and from single adherent cells (e.g., neurons) using micromanipulators.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for spatially resolved, in-vivo chemical analysis at the cellular scale, directly extending SPME's quantitative principles to previously inaccessible micro-environments. It will provide a critical tool for fundamental neurochemistry (e.g., measuring endocannabinoid flux at synapses) and for pharmaceutical development using microfluidic organ models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the extreme miniaturization of the complete SPME device to a sub-cellular scale while maintaining robust, quantifiable extraction performance, which has not been achieved in pre-2026 SPME work focused on tissue-level sampling. The integration of sequential, nanoscale coating on such a fine support is also unprecedented.

proposals.title: Quantitative Analysis of Flavor-Active Compounds in Complex Matrices via Stable Isotope Dilution Solid-Phase Microextraction Coupled to Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: Accurate quantification of flavor compounds is critical for food authenticity, quality control, and sensory science, but is hampered by matrix effects, analyte losses during preparation, and signal suppression/enhancement in mass spectrometry. Existing SPME methods, while excellent for sampling, can suffer from displacement effects and competition for fiber binding sites in complex samples, leading to quantification inaccuracies. Integrating a gold-standard quantification technique from clinical chemistry with advanced SPME could resolve these long-standing analytical challenges in flavor analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: That coupling stable isotope dilution analysis (SIDA) with sequential SPME using biocompatible, tailored coating materials will enable the precise and accurate quantification of key flavor compounds by correcting for matrix effects, displacement, and sample preparation losses.
proposals.method: We will synthesize or source deuterated or 13C-labeled analogues of target flavor compounds (e.g., esters, aldehydes, terpenes). These will be added as internal standards at the very beginning of sample preparation. Sequential SPME will be performed using recently developed biocompatible coatings (e.g., hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced particles in a stable binder) to address displacement. Extraction will be directly coupled to MS via a microfluidic open interface (MOI) for high-throughput analysis. Quantification will be based on the ratio of the native analyte to its isotopically labeled counterpart, using calibration curves constructed in representative food matrices.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, high-throughput SPME-MS protocol for absolute quantification of flavors, moving beyond semi-quantitative or relative measurements. It will provide a validated framework for applying SIDA to SPME, demonstrating its utility in correcting for competitive adsorption and matrix interferences. The methodology will be directly applicable to authentication studies and precision flavor chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While SIDA is established in other fields, its systematic integration with sequential, displacement-resistant SPME and direct MS interfacing for flavor analysis is novel. This approach uniquely tackles the combined challenges of accurate quantification and matrix complexity in a single, streamlined workflow.

proposals.title: Liquid-Phase Coated Microextraction Fibers: A Platform for Tunable and Dynamic Sampling via In Situ Solvent Exchange
proposals.motivation: Solid-phase microextraction (SPME) coatings, while robust, offer fixed extraction properties determined by their synthesis. This limits their adaptability for complex, variable matrices and hinders the on-demand tuning of selectivity and capacity. A liquid or gel stationary phase could enable dynamic control over extraction, addressing displacement effects and matrix challenges more flexibly than current solid coatings.
proposals.hypothesis: That a supported liquid or gel coating, where the extracting phase composition can be dynamically altered in situ via solvent exchange through a microfluidic interface, will provide tunable extraction kinetics and thermodynamics, outperforming static solid phases for quantitative analysis in complex and variable samples.
proposals.method: We will fabricate SPME fibers with a porous polymer binder (e.g., polyacrylonitrile) to immobilize a liquid extracting phase (e.g., ionic liquids, oligomers). This assembly will be integrated with a modified microfluidic open interface (MOI) to the mass spectrometer. The MOI will be adapted to deliver a solvent stream that can periodically re-condition or alter the liquid coating's composition between extractions. Extraction profiles will be monitored in real-time via direct-MS for model analytes (e.g., endocannabinoids, pharmaceuticals) to characterize tunability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm of dynamically tunable SPME, moving beyond static coatings. It will provide a methodological framework for on-demand adjustment of extraction selectivity and capacity, directly addressing quantitative challenges like displacement in complex tissues. The integration with direct-MS platforms will demonstrate a path toward intelligent, adaptive high-throughput analysis.
proposals.novelty: While biocompatible coatings address displacement, they remain solid phases. This proposal fundamentally challenges the need for a solid stationary phase by creating a dynamically reconfigurable liquid coating system, integrated with direct MS for real-time monitoring of property changes, which is not possible with pre-2026 SPME.

proposals.title: Development of a Heterogeneous Matrix-Compatible SPME Probe with a Segmented, Binder-Enhanced Coating for Direct, Quantitative Analysis of Solid and Viscous Samples
proposals.motivation: Conventional SPME methods assume homogeneous liquid or gaseous matrices, limiting direct application to complex, heterogeneous solids (e.g., tissues, soils, food) and viscous samples where analyte accessibility and coating-sample contact are non-uniform. Pre-2026, direct SPME analysis in such matrices faces challenges with poor reproducibility and significant displacement effects due to localized depletion and matrix-coating interactions, hindering accurate free concentration determination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a segmented SPME coating, incorporating a tailored polymeric binder phase to ensure robust, conformal contact with heterogeneous surfaces, will mitigate localized depletion and displacement effects, enabling direct, quantitative extraction from solid and viscous matrices.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate SPME fibers with a segmented coating architecture, where discrete extraction phases (e.g., C18, mixed-mode) are separated by inert spacers. A key innovation is the incorporation of a biocompatible, hydrogel-based binder polymer into the coating formulation to enhance adhesion and conformal contact with irregular solid surfaces. The method will be coupled to our established microfluidic open interface (MOI) for direct MS analysis. Performance will be validated using sequential SPME protocols on heterogeneous brain tissue (addressing endocannabinoids) and model viscous food samples, comparing results to homogenized liquid extracts.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new SPME probe design and protocol for direct analysis in traditionally intractable solid and viscous matrices, expanding the scope of SPME to intact samples. It will provide a fundamental framework for understanding and controlling extraction kinetics at heterogeneous interfaces, advancing quantitative in vivo and in situ analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly designing the SPME coating's physical architecture (segmentation) and chemical composition (integrated binder) to manage, rather than assume, sample heterogeneity, moving beyond the homogeneous matrix paradigm that has dominated SPME development.

proposals.title: On-Site Quantitative Analysis via Direct Coupling of Biocompatible SPME Fibers to Portable Mass Spectrometry Using a Miniaturized Microfluidic Open Interface
proposals.motivation: On-site environmental and forensic analysis requires rapid, quantitative determinations without the need for sample transport to a central lab. While portable mass spectrometers (MS) exist, their coupling with robust, quantitative sample preparation like SPME remains a challenge, often limited by complex interfaces and displacement effects in complex matrices. A seamless, field-deployable SPME-MS system would enable real-time, accurate measurements of target analytes in remote locations.
proposals.hypothesis: That a miniaturized, robust microfluidic open interface (MOI) can be developed to directly couple biocompatible SPME fiber coatings to a portable mass spectrometer, enabling rapid, quantitative on-site analysis by mitigating displacement effects through sequential extraction protocols.
proposals.method: The project will first design and fabricate a next-generation, miniaturized MOI with integrated heating and low-dead-volume fluidics optimized for portable MS systems. Biocompatible coatings (e.g., mixed-mode or restricted access materials) from recent sequential SPME work will be employed as the extraction phase. A field protocol will be developed involving timed, sequential extractions with the same fiber to address displacement, followed by direct thermal desorption into the MOI. The entire system control and data acquisition will be managed by a compact, battery-powered module running automated sequence and calibration algorithms.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully integrated, field-ready analytical platform combining the quantitative strengths of sequential SPME with the portability of MS. It will provide a validated methodology for on-site determination of free concentrations of small molecules (e.g., environmental contaminants, drugs) in complex liquid and semi-solid samples. The project will establish new benchmarks for speed and accuracy in deployable analytical chemistry.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of displacement-mitigating, sequential SPME protocols—a recent lab-based advancement—into a dedicated, ruggedized interface for portable MS, moving beyond proof-of-concept coupling to a system designed for reliable quantitative operation in the field.

proposals.title: Development of Sequential SPME with Zwitterionic and Mixed-Mode Coatings for the Quantitative Analysis of Highly Polar Metabolites and Small Peptides in Biological Fluids
proposals.motivation: The quantitative analysis of highly polar and ionic endogenous metabolites (e.g., amino acids, neurotransmitters) and small peptides remains a significant challenge for SPME due to their poor affinity for conventional hydrophobic coatings. This limitation hinders the application of SPME's key advantage—measuring free concentrations—to critical areas like metabolomics and neurochemistry. Addressing this gap is essential for non-depletive, in vivo, and high-throughput sampling of these biologically crucial compound classes.
proposals.hypothesis: That novel SPME coating materials, specifically zwitterionic polymers and mixed-mode sorbents incorporating ionic functionalities, can be engineered to provide sufficient and selective affinity for highly polar and ionic analytes, enabling their accurate quantification via sequential SPME protocols that account for displacement effects in complex matrices.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize a library of new biocompatible SPME fibers using sol-gel and binder-based techniques, focusing on zwitterionic sulfobetaine polymers and mixed-mode coatings combining C18 with weak cation/anion exchange groups. These fibers will be evaluated for their extraction efficiency and selectivity for model polar analytes (e.g., glutamate, acetylcholine, glutathione) in buffer and biological fluids. The optimized coatings will be integrated into a sequential SPME workflow, where two extractions from the same vial are performed to correct for displacement, and coupled to LC-MS/MS via a microfluidic open interface for high-throughput, sensitive analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new toolkit of SPME coatings explicitly designed for polar/ionic analytes, significantly expanding the domain of SPME applications. It will provide a validated, quantitative framework for measuring the free concentration of challenging metabolites and peptides, directly contributing to more accurate studies in systems biology and therapeutic monitoring. The project will also advance fundamental understanding of the interactions governing the extraction of highly polar compounds.
proposals.novelty: While sequential SPME for displacement correction is known, its combination with purpose-built zwitterionic and mixed-mode coatings for polar targets is novel. This approach moves beyond modifying existing hydrophobic phases, instead designing coatings from first principles based on the analyte's physicochemical properties, which is a non-obvious but necessary step for this compound class.

ideas.title: Closed-System Holistic Workflow for the Analysis of Ultra-Hydrophobic Compounds (log KOW > 5) via SPME-GC-MS
ideas.description: This research develops a complete, integrated analytical system designed for the reliable analysis of very hydrophobic organic compounds. It pairs optimized SPME fiber chemistry and extraction conditions with a fully coordinated, inert sample introduction and transfer pathway to prevent analyte loss post-extraction. The workflow specifically employs deactivated consumables, a solvent-assisted PTV injection strategy, and method protocols that minimize surface interactions. The result is a robust end-to-end solution that ensures quantitative analysis by overcoming the primary limitation of surface adsorption that plagues traditional methods for these compounds.

ideas.title: Chemometric-Biotic Calibration: Using Fish Bioconcentration Data to Train Multivariate Models for SPME Matrix Correction
ideas.description: This research fuses chemometric modeling with biota-based calibration to create a novel framework for environmental passive sampling. Known bioconcentration factors (BCFs) from fish for a suite of contaminants are used as the reference dataset to calibrate Solid-Phase Microextraction (SPME) fibers, treating the organism as the integrative sampler. Multivariate calibration models (e.g., PLS regression), adapted from food chemistry, are then trained on SPME fiber extraction profiles against these biotic reference concentrations. The resulting models can correct for complex environmental matrix effects in new samples and directly output estimates of the bioavailable fraction and potential bioconcentration, bridging chemical measurement and ecological risk assessment.

ideas.title: Large-scale SPME arrays for spatial mapping of bioavailable hydrophilic and ionizable contaminants
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the methodological pivot towards hydrophilic/ionizable compounds with the deployment of large-scale SPME arrays. It involves developing and deploying field-deployable arrays of SPME fibers specifically coated with novel sorbents (e.g., mixed-mode, hydrophilic-lipophilic balanced) designed to sequester bioavailable fractions of polar contaminants like pharmaceuticals, certain pesticides, and ionizable industrial chemicals. By deploying these arrays in water bodies, the approach generates spatially resolved maps of the freely dissolved concentration and chemical activity of these compounds, capturing gradients and hotspots influenced by local water chemistry (pH, ionic strength). This provides unprecedented field-scale data on the exposure risk posed by the bioavailable fraction of this challenging contaminant class, directly informing more accurate ecological and human health risk assessments.

ideas.title: Apply Pharmacokinetic Population Modeling to Correct SPME Signals for Environmental Matrix Effects
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from clinical pharmacokinetics, where population modeling corrects for biological matrix effects, to the problem of matrix effects in SPME analysis of environmental samples. In pharmacokinetics, nonlinear mixed-effects models disentangle true drug concentration from variability caused by patient physiology. Analogously, we propose to develop a chemometric model where environmental samples are treated as a population. The model would use calibration data from a diverse set of characterized environmental matrices to establish fixed effects for the core partitioning behavior of analytes onto the SPME fiber and random effects for the interference of specific matrix components. This would yield a robust calibration capable of correcting SPME signals in new, unknown environmental matrices, significantly improving quantitative accuracy without requiring perfect matrix matching.

ideas.title: Inverted Stable Isotope Dilution Analysis for Matrix-Robust Quantification of Yoghurt Metabolites
ideas.description: This research inverts the standard SIDA paradigm by using a stable isotope-labeled analog of a target yoghurt flavour compound as an 'external standard only' to create a primary calibration curve in a pure solvent. This curve is then used to quantify the native, unlabeled analyte in the complex yoghurt matrix. The key innovation is that the quantified native analyte then serves as a perfectly matrix-matched internal standard for the quantification of all other co-eluting, structurally similar flavour compounds via standard addition, using the original solvent-based calibration. This method synergistically combines the absolute precision of SIDA for one key compound with a novel, derivative internal standardization that grants the same level of matrix-robust accuracy to an entire related compound class, which neither approach could accomplish independently.

ideas.title: Challenging PDMS as the universal SPME fibre via a combinatorial materials screening approach
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a 100 μm PDMS fibre is the universal optimal choice for SPME by drawing an analogy from heterogeneous catalysis. In catalysis, the solution to finding the optimal catalyst for a specific reaction is high-throughput combinatorial screening of diverse material libraries, rather than relying on a single standard. Adapted to this context, the research idea involves creating a diverse library of potential SPME fibre coatings with varied chemical functionalities and structures. This library would then be screened in a parallel, automated fashion against a representative, complex sample matrix to rapidly identify the coating that provides superior selectivity and sensitivity for the target analytes, systematically moving beyond the default PDMS choice.

ideas.title: Inverse Modeling of Mixture Bioavailability and Toxicity from In Situ SPME Kinetics
ideas.description: This research direction applies SPME fibers to monitor the uptake kinetics of complex chemical mixtures and their degradation products directly in real environmental matrices (e.g., wastewater, sediment porewater). By developing inverse modeling frameworks on this kinetic data, the approach aims to deconvolute the combined bioavailability and predict the resulting baseline toxicity (or non-specific effects) of the entire dynamic mixture. This allows for the direct assessment of how transformation processes alter the overall bioavailable burden and toxic potential, providing a mechanistic link between in-situ chemical exposure and ecological effects for complex, evolving contaminant profiles.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmacokinetic Compartment Models to Predict SPME Sampling in Dynamic Water
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from pharmacology to the challenge of predicting Solid-Phase Microextraction (SPME) sampling rates in dynamic aquatic systems. In pharmacology, pharmacokinetic (PK) multi-compartment models successfully predict how a compound distributes from a central compartment (e.g., bloodstream) into peripheral tissues based on partition coefficients and clearance rates. Analogously, we can model a flowing water body as the central compartment and the SPME fiber as a peripheral tissue compartment. By adapting the mathematical framework of PK models, we can define uptake rate constants for the SPME fiber using the fiber/water partition coefficient (Kfw) and system-specific hydrodynamic parameters (e.g., flow velocity, boundary layer thickness). This approach provides a mechanistic model to calibrate passive SPME sampling in situ, moving beyond empirical calibrations and enabling accurate time-weighted average concentration measurements in rivers, estuaries, or industrial process waters where flow conditions are variable.

ideas.title: Adapting High-Throughput Screening Robotics from Pharmaceutical Discovery to SPME-Based Regulatory Testing
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the throughput bottleneck in regulatory SPME testing by drawing an analogy from pharmaceutical drug discovery. In that field, the challenge of screening vast chemical libraries was solved by developing integrated, robotic ultra-high-throughput screening (uHTS) platforms that use modular automation and multiplexed assay formats (e.g., 1536-well plates) to run parallel, synchronized workflows. We adapt this solution by designing a regulatory testing system where robotic autosamplers are integrated with multi-fiber SPME arrays (acting as the multiplexed format), automated sample carousels, and dedicated incubation/desorption modules. The entire process—from sample vial presentation and fiber exposure to thermal desorption—would be orchestrated by scheduling software to create a continuous, overlapping workflow. This adaptation of uHTS principles would transfer the high-throughput, parallel-processing capability from drug discovery to environmental or food safety testing, enabling a step-change in the number of samples processed per day for regulatory compliance.

ideas.title: Inverse modeling of baseline toxicity via SPME uptake kinetics using geophysical inversion methods
ideas.description: This research adapts the core methodology of geophysical seismic tomography to solve the inverse problem in environmental toxicology. In the analogous field, surface measurements of seismic wave travel times are used to inversely model subsurface structures. Here, the time-series measurement of chemical uptake by a Solid-Phase Microextraction (SPME) fiber serves as the surface signal. By constructing a physicochemical forward model of diffusion and partitioning to the fiber, an inverse calculation is performed to solve for the toxicologically critical parameters of the chemical—such as its baseline toxicity potential or bioconcentration factor—that would produce the observed kinetic signature. This approach allows for the prediction of hazard from a simple, non-destructive kinetic experiment, bypassing the need for equilibrium measurements or live organism testing.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Model-Informed Calibration of Passive Samplers Using Parallel Organism Exposure Systems
ideas.description: This approach calibrates SPME fibers by co-exposing them with live organisms in controlled microcosms, but avoids direct reliance on a single BCF value. Instead, it uses time-series bioaccumulation data from the organisms to parameterize a mechanistic partition-kinetic model that describes chemical exchange. The model, informed by real-time environmental sensors, predicts the freely dissolved concentration or chemical activity that the organism and SPME fiber are experiencing. The SPME fiber's response is then calibrated against this model-predicted activity, creating a robust, physics-based link that is validated by, but not dependent on, the organism's specific biological uptake.

ideas.title: Adapting Hydrophobic Surface Engineering from Marine Antifouling to SPME Fiber Design for Ultra-Hydrophobic Compounds
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from marine antifouling coatings—a field that solved the problem of strong, irreversible adsorption of hydrophobic organisms to surfaces—to the SPME challenge of extracting very hydrophobic compounds (log KOW > 5). The analogous solution involved engineering surfaces with amphiphilic polymer brushes or lubricant-infused nanostructures to create a thermodynamic barrier that weakens hydrophobic adhesion. The adapted idea is to develop and refine SPME protocols by designing novel fiber coatings that incorporate these principles. The goal is to create a 'low-adhesion' extraction phase that effectively captures hydrophobic analytes from aqueous samples but facilitates their complete and rapid release during thermal desorption, thereby overcoming the primary limitation of current coatings for such compounds.

ideas.title: Adapting GCxGC-TOFMS with Environmental Forensics Data Analysis for Yoghurt Flavoromics
ideas.description: The original idea proposes transferring GCxGC-TOFMS from petrochemicals to yoghurt flavor analysis for superior resolution. By analogy with environmental forensics—where the same technique is used to untangle complex pollutant mixtures—the adapted solution incorporates that field's advanced data analysis strategies. Specifically, we adapt the use of multivariate statistics (PCA, HCA) on GCxGC-TOFMS data to move beyond compound identification and create interpretable 'flavor fingerprints' for yoghurt. This allows for objective differentiation of products based on microbial activity, process parameters, and raw material quality, providing a powerful tool for dairy science and industrial quality assurance.

ideas.title: Develop and validate a kinetic SPME framework for dynamic chemical activity assessment in tiered regulatory ecotoxicology
ideas.description: This research moves beyond cataloging SPME drawbacks to actively constructing a next-generation regulatory tool. It develops a standardized kinetic SPME protocol to measure time-weighted average freely dissolved concentrations (Cfree) and uptake fluxes in complex environmental matrices, capturing dynamic exposure more accurately than equilibrium snapshots. The work will rigorously validate this framework against traditional methods across multiple model toxicants and species, demonstrating superior correlation with chronic toxicity endpoints. Finally, it will propose a concrete, tiered integration pathway for this kinetic SPME approach into existing OECD or EPA testing guidelines, providing a clear adoption roadmap for regulators.

ideas.title: Adapting Distributed Sensor Network Architecture for SPME Array Deployment
ideas.description: This idea adapts the distributed sensor network architecture from Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) in geophysics to solve the scaling challenge for SPME-based environmental monitoring. In DAS, a single fiber optic cable acts as thousands of seismic sensors via backscatter analysis. The adapted solution proposes replacing discrete SPME fibers with a continuous, robust cable or tape coated with a multi-analyte SPME sorbent phase. This 'SPME line' would be deployed in situ across a water body. A field-portable automated analysis unit would then move along the line, performing localized thermal desorption and chromatographic analysis at programmed intervals. This creates a high-resolution, distributed chemical sensing array from a single, scalable deployment, dramatically improving the spatial mapping efficiency for bioavailable contaminants compared to managing individual fiber arrays.

ideas.title: Adapting Radioactive Tracer Dilution for Internal Standard Quantification
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that external calibration alone is sufficient for accurate quantification by drawing an analogy from hydrology's tracer dilution methods. In hydrology, a known tracer is introduced to a system to measure flow by its dilution, correcting for complex environmental variables. The adapted research idea is to implement an internal standard not merely as a reference peak, but as a quantitative 'chemical tracer' spiked into each sample at a fixed concentration before any processing. The analyte concentration would then be determined by the precise ratio of its response to the tracer's response, calibrated against standards containing both. This directly corrects for losses during sample preparation, injection volume inconsistencies, and matrix effects, fundamentally inverting the reliance on an external standard-only framework to a tracer-corrected internal standard quantification system.

ideas.title: A Tiered Framework for Rational Internal Standard Selection and Qualification to Replace External-Only Calibration
ideas.description: This study proposes a systematic framework to replace the simplistic 'internal vs. external' standard decision. The framework guides analysts through a decision tree based on analyte chemistry, matrix complexity, and instrument platform to select the optimal standard type—stable isotope-labeled, structural analogue, or external. Each candidate internal standard must then pass a quantitative qualification protocol that verifies its correction capability for specific error sources like extraction recovery and ion suppression. This ensures that the switch to an internal standard method is data-driven and robust, genuinely improving accuracy rather than introducing new vulnerabilities.

ideas.title: Hydrodynamic Calibration of SPME Sampling Rates Using Dimensionless Mass Transfer Correlations for Dynamic Aquatic Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a first-principles, hydrodynamic calibration framework for SPME in aquatic environments, moving beyond analogies to air models. It establishes empirical Sherwood number correlations for common SPME geometries (e.g., fiber, blade) as a function of Reynolds and Schmidt numbers through controlled flume experiments. This physics-based model directly accounts for the turbulent flow and boundary layer conditions specific to water, enabling the prediction of sampling rates across a wide range of field-relevant hydrodynamic regimes. The approach is validated through field deployments in streams and coastal waters, comparing SPME uptake against time-integrated active sampling measurements.

ideas.title: A Drift-Robust, Metabolite-Informed Electronic Nose System for Quantitative Yoghurt Flavour Profiling
ideas.description: This redesigned system combines a commercial electronic nose with a standardized sampling chamber and a dedicated analytics module. The core innovation is a hybrid machine learning model trained on a foundational dataset that aligns sensor array outputs with both human sensory evaluations and analytical chemistry data (GC-MS). This allows the system to output not just a fingerprint, but quantitative estimates of specific desirable (e.g., diacetyl, acetaldehyde) and undesirable volatile compounds, corrected for sensor drift and environmental variables. The result is a rapid, routine-compatible tool that provides actionable, interpretable flavour quality metrics directly on the production floor.

ideas.title: Applying a 'Standard Reference Material' Framework to Yogurt Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a method devised for natural, set yoghurts cannot be directly extended to stirred or flavoured varieties. By drawing an analogy from analytical chemistry's use of Standard Reference Materials (SRMs) to validate methods for complex matrices, we propose a systematic validation framework. The solution involves creating a series of well-characterized 'reference yogurt matrices' representing the target varieties. The original method is then applied to these matrices to empirically determine which parameters (e.g., viscosity from stirring, pH shifts from fruit, optical properties from coloring) cause deviation. The outcome is not a new method for each type, but a calibrated understanding of the original method's robustness, potentially leading to a simple decision tree, pre-treatment steps, or mathematical corrections that allow its reliable application across a broader range of yogurt products, thereby significantly expanding its utility.

ideas.title: Challenge the 'recombined milk' baseline in yoghurt studies via a multi-substrate experimental framework
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecology and soil science, where treatments are tested across a standardized panel of diverse soil substrates to avoid biased conclusions, this idea adapts a 'multi-substrate' experimental framework to dairy research. The analogous solution is to challenge the common use of recombined milk as the sole or baseline source in yoghurt studies by systematically comparing it against a controlled panel of other milks (fresh, UHT, different species, varied compositions) under identical fermentation conditions. The adapted research idea is to implement this framework, treating milk type as the variable substrate, to rigorously test the assumption that results from recombined milk are generalizable. This will explicitly reveal how the intrinsic properties of the milk matrix—beyond just its gross nutrient composition—govern microbial metabolism, gelation, and final product quality, leading to more nuanced and applicable insights in fermented dairy science.

ideas.title: Controlled deconstruction of recombination: Isolating the effects of ingredient reassembly and processing on yogurt gel properties and fermentation dynamics
ideas.description: This study systematically isolates the impact of milk recombination on yogurt quality by using a tightly controlled formulation and processing framework. A fresh milk control is compositionally matched by reconstituting skim milk powder and anhydrous milk fat in water, creating a 'recombined' treatment with identical gross composition. Both milk types are then subjected to identical, precisely documented homogenization and heat treatment regimes. The primary analysis measures the fermentation kinetics (pH decline, acidification rate) and the physical properties of the final gel (storage modulus G', loss tangent, water holding capacity) to test the specific hypothesis that the recombination process, even with matched composition, alters protein functionality and gel microstructure. This approach controls for confounding variables to directly attribute any differences to the physical-chemical consequences of drying, storage, and rehydration of milk components.
